summary: Social Circle approved a 90-day moratorium on new data center applications after being overwhelmed with seven applications. Despite the moratorium, the council approved a controversial rezoning for 151 acres, drawing resident opposition. Part of a statewide wave of Georgia moratoriums. Opposition was led by One Circle Community Coalition. The community achieved a partial victory in its effort.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Social Circle (Multiple)

summary: Google withdrew its 468-acre 'Project Flo' data center in Franklin Township, Indianapolis on September 22, 2025, hours before the City-County Council was expected to vote it down. The 'Protect Franklin Township' campaign gathered over 8,000 petition signatures and invested in digital billboard ads across Marion County. Fifteen of 25 council members announced opposition, citing massive water and electricity consumption for just a handful of permanent jobs. About 60 protestors gathered outside the September 8 council meeting, and loud cheers erupted at news of the withdrawal.
objective: Community pressure forces Google to withdraw in Franklin Township

summary: City of South Fulton approved a data center ordinance in June 2025 with criteria for noise levels and residential distance. In August 2025, the council approved T5 Data Centers' ATL IV campus: a 91-acre, three-building, 1.32M sq ft facility supporting 200 MW of IT load. T5 finalized land acquisition August 28, 2024. An on-site substation will be operational by 2026, with Phase 1 delivery by 2027. The city estimates $7-10M annually in revenue for parks, public services, and community programs. Predominantly Black communities in South Fulton and Union City continue to effort against at least 20 planned data centers in the area.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in City of South Fulton

summary: Menomonie signed an NDA with Balloonist LLC in February 2024, over a year before announcing the $1.6B data center proposal in July 2025. Over the following six months, residents coordinated a local opposition campaign. Mayor Randy Knaack announced on Sept. 22 that the city was no longer interested in pursuing the project. On January 5, 2026, the City Council revised a zoning ordinance to bar Balloonist from moving ahead. The data center would have used approximately 75,000 gallons of water per day and brought 50-75 full-time jobs. The project involves 750 MW capacity, 324 acres.
objective: Oppose Balloonist data center in Menomonie

project_name: Lansing AI Data Center (former Milliken Station)
summary: FLX Strong and Cayuga Lake Environmental Action Now (CLEAN) filed an Article 78 petition on January 29, 2026 in Tompkins County Supreme Court against the Town of Lansing Zoning Board of Appeals, TeraWulf Inc., its subsidiary Lake Hawkeye, property owner Cayuga Operating Company, and applicant Fred DelFavero. The suit challenges the ZBA's narrow 3-2 vote on December 22, 2025 that classified TeraWulf's proposed 400MW data center at the former Milliken Station coal plant as a 'general processing facility' permitted in the industrial zone. The groups allege ZBA 'effectively rewritten the Town Code under the guise of interpretation' and cite 'bias, legal defects, and procedural irregularities.' Previously, Lansing's Town Board withdrew a proposed one-year moratorium in November 2025 after TeraWulf threatened legal action over alleged Open Meetings Law violations.
objective: Legal challenge to TeraWulf data center in Lansing

opposition_groups: Taylor community residents (led by Carrie D'Anna)
Halt the BPP Project in Taylor
summary: Five Taylor residents sued to stop a 135,000 sq ft data center with an on-site electrical substation on land deeded in 1999 as parkland. Judge Ryan Larson initially issued a temporary block but dismissed the case on Oct. 8, 2025, ruling residents lacked standing. Construction is proceeding. The Blueprint Data Centers project involves a 60 MW capacity, $1 billion in investment, 52 acres, 135,000 square feet of building space. The developer promised 30 permanent jobs. Opposition was led by Griffin family plaintiffs, Taylor community residents (led by Carrie D'Anna), and Halt the BPP Project in Taylor.
objective: Legal challenge to Blueprint Data Centers data center in Taylor

summary: Fidelis New Energy proposed the 2,300+ acre Mountaineer GigaSystem (hydrogen + data center) campus in Mason County, WV. In March 2026, California-based Nscale acquired the project (Monarch Compute Campus) by buying American Intelligence and Power Corporation. Nscale immediately signed a memorandum of understanding with Microsoft to provide 1.35 GW of capacity for an AI data center using NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72 GPUs. Once fully built, capacity could reach 1GW (potentially expanding further), with up to $5B invested. WV state law HB 2014 stripped local zoning authority over data centers. Residents continue to raise concerns about water usage, noise, emissions, and lack of community input. First phase expected operational in 2028.
objective: Cap data center water consumption in WV

summary: Delaware HB 233 (Rep. Frank Burns / Sen. Stephanie Hansen) requires PSC-regulated utilities like Delmarva Power to establish a separate rate for large energy-use facilities (≥20 MW used primarily for web hosting, streaming, or data processing). HB 233 cleared a House committee in January 2026 and was scheduled for a full House vote in February. Evaluation factors include rate impact on other customers, equitable contributions to grid reliability, ability to meet renewable energy targets, and impact on greenhouse gas reduction. The bill responds to fears that data center proliferation could strain Delaware's grid and raise residential bills. Separately, the controversial Delaware City data center proposal faced a major setback when its environmental approval was denied in February 2026 — the denial was upheld by a state board in March 2026.
objective: Regulate data center development in Delaware

summary: Shutesbury Special Town Meeting approved a 9-month moratorium bylaw on data centers on September 30, 2025. Planning Board voted 6-1 to bring it forward after a potential company expressed interest. Shutesbury is among Franklin County, MA towns being evaluated in a data center feasibility study. Massachusetts opposition to data centers has grown statewide in 2026, with Lowell passing a yearlong moratorium and Everett residents pushing for one. However, Gov. Maura Healey has come out in support of data centers (unlike Maine's Mills), creating tension with grassroots opposition. As of early 2026, no formal data center proposal has been filed in Shutesbury.
objective: Impose 9-month data centers moratorium in Shutesbury

opposition_groups: White County IN Against Data Centers
summary: White County's Board of Commissioners passed a moratorium on data center development on October 20, 2025, after residents packed public meetings in opposition. The moratorium was prompted by a proposed project near Wolcott. Residents voiced concerns about water availability and process transparency. White County joined Marshall (February) and Putnam (November) as Indiana's three counties with data center moratoriums, which conflict with a state government actively courting tech projects through a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases. The project involves 287 acres.
objective: Halt data centers in White County after Undisclosed proposal

summary: A developer proposed a backup data center at Kuiper Farm (2400 55th Drive) in the Village of Yorkville, about five miles from Microsoft's AI data center under construction in Mount Pleasant. The Yorkville Village Board and Plan Commission held a joint pre-application conference on December 8, 2025 and made clear the village had no interest in hosting a data center. The developer said they would move on if there was no community support. Microsoft stated it was 'not currently evaluating any sites for data center development in Yorkville.' This was Microsoft's second failed attempt at a backup data center in Racine County, after withdrawing a 244-acre proposal in neighboring Caledonia following a 2,000-signature petition. The project involves the Ken Nelson proposal.
objective: Reject Microsoft-linked backup data center proposal in Village of Yorkville

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Jones County
No Data Center in Jones County GA
summary: EagleRock Partners withdrew its rezoning application for the 600+ acre Crooked Creek Technology Park after organized opposition under the 'No Data Centers in Jones County' campaign. Commissioners voted 4-1 for a 90-day moratorium to study data center impacts and develop regulations. The county later rescinded several data center-related zoning ordinances after determining residents had not been given proper legal notice of the changes, further delaying any future data center development. The project involves $5B investment, 600 acres.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Jones County (EagleRock Partners)

opposition_groups: Concerned Citizens of Twiggs County (Rob Fricks, attorney)
Protect Twiggs County -- No Data Center
summary: Twiggs County commissioners unanimously approved Eagle Rock Partners' $2B data center rezoning of ~300 acres (Weyerhaeuser-owned land) on September 18, 2025, despite hundreds of residents' objections. Residents filed a lawsuit in Twiggs County Superior Court alleging procedural violations: the notice sign was placed on the wrong property with an incorrect hearing date, and the commission did not acquire a required impact report before voting. The county's December response denied the allegations. As of February 2026, a judge is reviewing the case and is expected to issue a written decision.
objective: Challenge EagleRock Partners rezoning in Twiggs County in court

project_name: Columbia North Industrial Rezoning (potential data center)
summary: A landowner sought to rezone 63 acres of farmland near U.S. 63 for a potential data center. The Planning & Zoning Commission voted 5-2 to recommend the change, but the City Council rejected it 3-3 on Nov. 19, 2025. Residents objected to the loss of farmland and questioned why the project couldn't use already-industrial land. The project involves 200 MW capacity.
objective: Block Undisclosed rezoning of 63 acres of farmland

summary: City Council voted 5-2 to sell 500 acres of recreational land to Huntley LLC for a $10B, 1,000 MW data center — less than one mile from Horseshoe Bend. 'Page Residents for Voter Rights' collected 405 signatures to force a referendum, but it was disqualified on a technicality (numbering on pages). A recall effort targeting Mayor Kidman and Councillor Roundtree is ongoing. The project involves 1 GW capacity.
objective: Recall officials who approved Huntley data center

summary: Over 30 Carroll County residents filed a lawsuit in Superior Court challenging Villa Rica's annexation and rezoning of 200+ acres for Embry Development Company's proposed Avemore Data Park. Door-to-door petitions gathered 500 signatures with approximately 95% of respondents voicing concerns about the project. Concerns include 1.1 million gallons/day water use and proximity to residential areas. The lawsuit is ongoing. The project involves $5B investment, 400 acres.
objective: Challenge Embry Development Company rezoning in Villa Rica in court

summary: Over 100 residents from New York and New Jersey objected to DataBank's proposal for a 146,000 sq-ft, 45 MW data center adjacent to Lake Tappan reservoir in Orangetown, New York. The Bi-State Datacenter Crisis Coalition organized opposition citing threats to wetlands, wildlife, water quality, and fire risks from lithium-ion battery storage. The project remains under review. The project involves 34 acres.
objective: Organize coalition against DataBank data center in Orangetown

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Preble County Planning Commission unanimously (11-0) denied rezoning of 300 acres of farmland for a data center. The developer withdrew his application in October after residents organized a referendum campaign. Former Republican state lawmaker Gene Krebs helped lead opposition. Opposition was led by Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio. The project involves the Patrick Scanlon proposal.
objective: Deny Patrick Scanlon (developer) rezoning of 300 acres of farmland

summary: Trenton (Butler County, OH) City Planning Commissioners voted unanimously in late March 2026 to approve Prologis' site plan for Project Mila, an 880,000 sq ft data center development on 141 acres in Trenton Industrial Park. The project includes 4 buildings (each 220,000 sq ft) plus an electrical substation. Projected 140 jobs. Construction on the first building could start later in 2026 for a 2028 launch, with all four buildings complete by 2029. Prologis says it will pay for all required power system upgrades and utility costs. Water/sewer/stormwater fees could bring $120,000/year to the city. Community pushback continues over water usage, energy bills, and noise impacts. Technical components like stormwater management remain under review.
objective: Pause or deny Prologis data center site plan in Trenton Industrial Park

summary: Mason County, Kentucky, planning commission approved a zoning ordinance paving the way for a 2,080-acre data center campus north of AA Highway, west of Maysville, by an undisclosed Fortune 100 company under NDA. The 'We Are Mason County' grassroots group filed a lawsuit on March 27, 2026 against the Mason County Fiscal Court and Planning Commission challenging the rezoning of 28 properties. Separately, farmer Ida Huddleston and daughter Delsia Bare rejected ~$26 million in combined offers to sell 534 acres of family farmland to the anonymous developer. At contentious March 25-26 rezoning hearings, residents pressed the developer's attorney on the company's identity, which has only been shared with officials bound by NDAs. The majority of hearing speakers objected to the project.
objective: Oppose 2,000-acre data center ordinance adoption in Mason County via We Are Mason County coalition

summary: Project Kestrel, a $100B multi-phase data center campus by Shenandoah Computing LLC (linked to Google), proposes six hyperscale data centers totaling 1.8 million sq ft on 379 acres at the KCI-29 mega-site north of Kansas City International Airport. PortKC received a conduit bond application that could reach $100B with property tax incentives for up to 35 years per phase. Phase 1 construction is set to begin in 2026, with full build-out over 20 years. Expected to generate $110M+ in total tax revenue and ~50 high-paying jobs per phase. Platte County Presiding Commissioner has publicly objected to the project.
objective: Study data center impacts on water resources in MO

project_name: Lower Mt. Bethel Data Center Campus
summary: Peron Development and J.G. Petrucci Company are proposing a data center campus on approximately 450 acres at the intersection of Gravel Hill Road and Martins Creek Belvidere Highway in Lower Mt. Bethel Township, Northampton County. The site is across from the John M. Mauser Nature Education Trail System along the scenic Delaware River. A petition gathered ~4,600 signatures voicing concerns about the project. The proposal remains under review with no formal approval as of early 2026.
objective: 4600-signature petition opposing Peron Development data center

opposition_groups: Joplin Citizens Against Wildwood Ranch Data Center Site
summary: The Joplin City Council approved annexation and rezoning of Wildwood Ranch for a data center. The Joplin Sustainable Technology Alliance launched a referendum effort but fell short, collecting only about half of the ~8,000 signatures needed by the March 7 deadline. On March 17, 2026, the Council certified the petitions as insufficient. The annexation and rezoning of the 600-acre Wildwood Ranch site stands. The site, owned by local developer Jimmer Pinjuv, offers 200+ MW capacity on the southwest side of the city. The project involves 100 MW capacity, 540 acres.
objective: Halt data centers in Joplin after Jimmer Pinjuv / Wildwood Ranch proposal

summary: Despite a petition with 2,500+ signatures and a 4,000-member opposition group, Hobart reached a deal with Amazon Web Services for a data center on 500+ acres. AWS plans to invest $15 billion in 2.4 GW of data center capacity across Northwest Indiana. The project was approved over community objections. The No Data Centers Hobart Indiana group has launched a dedicated opposition website with legal and community action resources. The project involves 500 acres.
objective: Petition to block Amazon/AWS data center in Hobart

summary: RadiusDC is planning to build two 100,000 sq ft data centers in Plainfield using 24 MW of electricity and about 700 gallons of water/day. The Plainfield Plan Commission unanimously approved the primary plat petition on Jan. 5, 2026, after more than a dozen concerned residents objected to the project during public comment. A Change.org petition opposes the project citing lack of transparency. The project involves 31 acres.
objective: Petition to block RadiusDC data center in Plainfield

company: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC / BHGH Properties LLC
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Lordstown was one of the first Ohio municipalities to institute a moratorium on data centers. In October 2025, developer Bristolville 25 Developer LLC submitted site plans for a 133-acre, $3.6B data center project spanning Trumbull and Mahoning counties. In November 2025, the council initially passed an outright ban on data centers, then rescinded it after legal advice and replaced it with a 180-day moratorium. Bristolville 25 filed a lawsuit in the Ohio Supreme Court in November 2025, alleging the village failed to properly review their proposal and is using the moratorium to squash the project. In a March 9, 2026 filing, Bristolville argued the mandamus should still be issued since Lordstown imposed the moratorium only after the lawsuit was filed. The project would create 1,600 construction jobs and 120 permanent positions with an average hourly wage over $84.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Lordstown (Bristolville 25 Developer)

company: Ohio Industrial Realty Group (potential tenant: Amazon)
opposition_groups: Save the IX Center Campaign (Cassia Core, organizer)
summary: A petition led by resident Cassia Core gathered nearly 2,000 signatures voicing concerns about Ohio Industrial Realty Group's proposed conversion of the historic Cleveland IX Center into a 25 MW AI data center, with Amazon reportedly a potential tenant. The IX Center has hosted the Cleveland Auto Show and Christmas Connection for decades. Brook Park residents are particularly vocal about noise pollution and utility price increases. The proposal remains under review. The project involves 90 acres.
objective: 2000-signature petition opposing Ohio Industrial Realty Group data center

project_name: Cleveland Cliffs Steel Plant Data Center
summary: A developer attempted to convert the Cleveland Cliffs steel plant on 66 acres of Plymouth Township's riverfront corridor into a data center for undisclosed Big Tech tenants. Over 1,000 people signed a petition voicing concerns about the project. The application was abruptly withdrawn in November 2025 due to legal issues regarding the developer's standing. The project involves the Brian O'Neill proposal, $9B investment.
objective: Petition to block Brian O'Neill (developer) data center in Conshohocken /

summary: Lackawanna County Commissioner Bill Gaughan asked Governor Shapiro and the PA General Assembly for a three-year statewide moratorium on new large-scale data center approvals. The county alone has at least 11 proposed data center campuses, six in Archbald. State Senator Rosemary Brown announced support for a moratorium and unveiled a legislative package to restrict data centers to industrial zones. State Representatives Kyle Mullins and Kyle Donahue also back the legislation. The project involves the 1778 Rich Pike LLC proposal, 1500 MW capacity, $14.25B investment, 1,000 acres.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after 1778 Rich Pike proposal

summary: The $7B Stargate datacenter in Saline Township faces strong community response from residents and environmental groups. Over 5,000 public comments were submitted to the Michigan Public Service Commission, with the vast majority objected to. Residents call the project 'uniquely evil' for destroying wetlands, straining the grid, and reshaping their farming community. Legal challenges allege Open Meetings Act violations in the settlement that allowed the project to proceed. DTE's 1.4GW power contract was approved 3-0 despite opposition. The project involves the Oracle proposal, 575 acres.
objective: Sue over open meeting violations in OpenAI / Oracle approval

company: Franks Investment Company LLC / SAC III Acquisition Co
summary: Shreveport City Council voted 7-0 on December 18, 2025 to approve a special use permit for a 2.8 million sq ft data center at Resilient Technology Park (7340 Greenwood Road), overturning the Metropolitan Planning Commission's December 3 denial. The project by Franks Investment Company LLC / SAC III Acquisition Co includes seven two-story data center buildings, a 104,000 sq ft office building, and an on-site electrical substation. Expected to create 150-200 full-time jobs at ~$85,000/year and generate $20M in local tax revenue over three years. More than 50 public comment cards were submitted and several protesters were escorted out.
objective: Legal challenge to Franks Investment Company data center in Shreveport

summary: Thor Equities proposed a $1B data center on 810+ acres in Augusta Township near Milan, and the Board of Trustees unanimously approved rezoning 522 acres from Agricultural Residential to General Industrial in July 2025. Residents formed Protect Augusta Charter Township (PACT) and submitted 957 petition signatures for a referendum, which was certified in September 2025. The referendum was placed on the August 4, 2026 ballot, where voters will decide whether to repeal the rezoning. Opponents cite noise, pollution, and higher electric rates. The project involves 100 MW capacity, 800 acres.
objective: Petition to block Thor Equities data center in Milan

summary: A proposal to convert the former Pennhurst State School site in Chester County into a 1.3 million sq ft data center campus (five two-story buildings, substation, and solar field) drew massive opposition with 12,000+ petition signatures. In December 2025, the board of supervisors scrapped a draft ordinance to restrict data centers. In February 2026, the planning commission voted to recommend the board of supervisors deny the proposal, calling it 'technically deficient' and not in compliance with zoning. A final hearing before the board of supervisors was scheduled for March 2026.
opposition_groups: Prevent East Vincent Data Center Development
objective: Deny Penn Hurst Holdings DE data center on 125 acres in East Vincent

summary: Change.org petition with 216 signatures voicing concerns about data centers in Temple. Rowan Digital Infrastructure received unanimous city council approval for a $700 million, 300 MW hyperscale data center campus on 700 acres along Bob White Road. Construction started fall 2025 with 12-18 month timeline. The facility borders Robinson Family Farm. Rowan says it will use a closed-loop cooling system with minimal water consumption (1-2 million gallons filled once, lasting 10-15 years). Meta also previously paused an $800 million data center project in Temple in 2022. Community concerns center on energy consumption and infrastructure impact.
objective: 216-signature petition opposing Rowan Digital Infrastructure data center

summary: Gordon County enacted a preemptive data center moratorium, despite no active data center proposals. County officials studied ordinances in Jackson, Spalding, and Wayne counties to develop regulations for the design, construction, operation, and remediation of data centers. Officials said the goal is to ensure that 'when data centers come, we will get the benefits of them, but not the burdens.' The county near Calhoun determined it needs specific regulations before any data center development can proceed.
objective: Pause data centers in Gordon County pending regulations

summary: Albemarle County proposed a zoning overlay for data centers that would designate five areas as appropriate. The Piedmont Environmental Council raised concerns. The county paused the process in October 2025 to evaluate potential legislation from the 2026 Virginia General Assembly session. The community achieved a partial victory in its effort.
objective: Create data center overlay zone in Albemarle County

summary: Jordan Harmon and Mackenzie Roberts (both Muscogee Nation citizens) organized town halls after plans emerged to build a massive AI data center on 5,500 acres of Muscogee Nation land (the Looped Square Ranch near Okmulgee) originally purchased for food security, hunting, and fishing. Elected officials were required to sign NDAs preventing them from sharing project details with constituents. The Muscogee Nation National Council voted 4-11 to reject the proposal (NCA 25-077) on November 15, 2025. Featured on the cover of TIME magazine in February 2026. The project involves 5,500 acres.
objective: Deny Undisclosed data center on 5500 acres in Muscogee

summary: Google confirmed a $4B, 600 MW data center on 1,178 acres in West Memphis, Arkansas ('Project Pyramid'). Critics raised concerns about water, noise, utility rate increases, and that 1,100 acres support only 300 jobs. Arkansas Act 373 allows utilities to pass infrastructure costs to ratepayers. The project was approved despite opposition. The project involves 1,178 acres.
objective: Oppose Google $4B data center over water and rate impacts

summary: Dundee Township enacted a 90-day moratorium on data center development in October 2025 in response to Cloverleaf Infrastructure's proposal. The moratorium was extended six months in January 2026, carrying it through mid-2026. During the moratorium, no new applications can be submitted, and anything already in process is frozen. The township board is gathering information before taking a formal position. At least 27 Michigan communities now have active data center moratoria. State Rep. Jennifer Wortz (R-Quincy) introduced legislation to halt data center construction statewide until April 2027.
opposition_groups: Citizens Against a Dundee Data Center
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Dundee Township (Cloverleaf Infrastructure)

summary: The City of Bristol unanimously approved a 2-year moratorium on data processing centers in September-October 2025, passing on both readings. The moratorium will remain in effect through 2027 while the city researches comprehensive zoning regulations including industrial zoning amendments, residential setbacks, utility needs (electricity/cooling), building styles, and community input requirements. The city had no existing zoning regulations for data centers before the moratorium.
objective: Impose 2-year data centers moratorium in City of Bristol

summary: Microsoft cancelled its 244-acre, 900 MW Caledonia data center (Project Nova) after a 2,000-signature petition and strong community opposition forced a rethinking of the plan. Microsoft said it remains committed to the region and Racine County officials are working to identify an alternative site, possibly still in Caledonia. Microsoft's other Wisconsin projects (Mount Pleasant, Kenosha) are unaffected. The project involves $7B investment.
objective: Petition to block Microsoft data center in Village of Caledonia

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Ozaukee County
summary: Residents in Fredonia and Saukville formed a joint coalition to challenge new power-line routes serving the Vantage Data Center planned for Port Washington. The 'Responsible Energy Alliance' (~500 members) and the 'Protect Fredonia Coalition' (chaired by Pastor Patti Plough) are pushing back against against American Transmission Company's proposed routes of 96-123 miles of new transmission lines and up to 5 new substations. ATC submitted its application to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission. The PSC will decide on routes in spring 2026. The project involves the Vantage Data Centers proposal, 1300 MW capacity, $15B investment, 672 acres.
objective: Commission legislative study on data center impacts in WI

summary: California SB 57 directs the CPUC to assess whether investor-owned utility costs from new data center loads result in cost shifts to other retail customers. Report is due to the Legislature by January 1, 2027. Signed by Governor Newsom on October 11, 2025 (Chapter 647, Statutes of 2025). The law takes a study-only approach; earlier prescriptive provisions were removed.
objective: Prevent data center cost pass-through to residential rates in CA

opposition_groups: Meade County Residents Against Data Centers
summary: Thomas A. Hobbs Living Trust requested rezoning of a 138-acre site along Joe Prather Highway (25025 Joe Prather Hwy, Ekron) from Agricultural/Residential to Light Industrial for data center development in Meade County, Kentucky. After a 5-hour public hearing with 150+ residents and a 2,800-signature petition, the Planning Commission declined to recommend rezoning. Fiscal Court unanimously blocked the proposal and subsequently adopted a moratorium (Ordinance 2025-07) on new data center and IT infrastructure facility applications.
objective: Deny Thomas A. Hobbs Living Trust rezoning of 138 acres of farmland

summary: Manchester Township enacted a 2-year data center moratorium beginning October 14, 2025 — one of the longest moratorium durations among Michigan communities. By Feb 2026, at least 27 Michigan communities had active moratoria as part of a wider push that has now reached the State Capitol where a bipartisan group of lawmakers introduced a three-bill package to temporarily halt new data center development across Michigan. Despite township opposition, the high-profile Stargate project in nearby Saline Township is moving toward 2026 groundbreaking.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
objective: Impose 2-year data centers moratorium in Manchester Township

opposition_groups: Stop the Pine Island Data Center
summary: Google is building a data center on 482 acres called 'Project Skyway' in Pine Island, with Ryan Companies as the developer. MCEA filed suit October 16, 2025, arguing the city's Alternative Urban Areawide Review was legally insufficient because officials and Ryan Companies knew the project was being developed specifically for Google but omitted critical details about size, scale, and impacts on energy and water. Google's identity was revealed February 24, 2026; the company says the 250,000 sq ft facility will use advanced air-cooling technology and not consume water for cooling. The case will be heard in Goodhue County District Court on April 6, 2026.
objective: Sue for environmental review of Google project

summary: The proposed 1.8 million sq ft 'Project Loon' hyperscale data center was kept secret from the public until September 2025, despite city officials discussing initial details as early as September 2024. Hermantown changed land use plans and approved a preliminary environmental review before the public knew it was a data center. On Oct. 20, the City Council voted 4-0 to rezone 200+ acres, with nearly 300 attending and dozens testifying against. The 'Stop the Hermantown Data Center' Facebook group gained 3,500+ members. SHDC (a non-profit LLC) partnered with MCEA to appeal the city's environmental study. Residents launched lawn signs, canvassing, and door-knocking campaigns. On March 3, 2026, Google was confirmed as the company behind the controversial 1.8-million-sq-ft, 700MW data center proposal in Hermantown. Minnesota Power signed an electric service agreement with Google. The project faces a pending lawsuit from the Stop the Hermantown Data Center group. The Hermantown Area Chamber of Commerce called it 'one of the most significant private investments ever considered' in the region. The project involves the Mortenson proposal, $650M investment, 403 acres.
objective: Sue for environmental review of Mortenson / Harmony Group project

summary: Directs electric public utilities to file a new tariff for large load customers (100+ MW/month) with the BPU, designed to ensure non-data-center ratepayers are protected from increased costs caused by massive electricity demand. Gov. Murphy pocket-vetoed the original bill before leaving office in January 2026. Reintroduced in the new session as S731/A796, which passed the New Jersey General Assembly with bipartisan support.
bill_name: A5462 / S4307 - Large Load Customer Tariff
objective: Create large-load utility tariff for 100MW+ data centers (A5462)

summary: Logan County Commissioners unanimously approved a 180-day moratorium on solar, wind, data center, and battery energy storage development in October 2025 after Granite Renewables (New York-based, develops co-located data centers) began visiting county officials. The potential project involves up to 2 GW of electricity, a $15B capital investment, 1,000 construction jobs, and 50-100 permanent positions. Commissioners expressed a desire for economic development but wanted to avoid hasty decisions. Water availability from the South Platte River is a key concern.
objective: Impose 180-day battery energy storage systems moratorium in Logan County

summary: Douglas County imposed a 90-day data center moratorium in a 4-1 vote in March 2025, later extended in August 2025 to study ratepayer impacts. DC BLOX closed $1.15B in green loan financing in August 2025 for its 120MW Atlanta West campus on 54 acres in Lithia Springs, adjacent to Microsoft and Google facilities. DC BLOX Phase 1 delivery delayed to 2026. Separately, a company filed an application for 11 buildings and three warehouse data centers in western Douglas County. Planning officials raised concerns about ratepayer costs for infrastructure upgrades.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Douglas County (DC BLOX)

summary: Stafford County adopted Virginia's strictest data center regulations in a 4-2-1 vote on October 22, 2025, including a 750-foot setback from residences (up from 100 ft), 200-foot vegetative buffer (up from 50 ft), 55 dB noise cap, and post-occupancy noise evaluations annually for 10 years. Five already-approved projects (Stafford Technology Campus, Potomac Church Tech Center, Centreport Gateway/Pemberton, Vantage VA-4, Cranes Corner) were grandfathered in December 2025. The regulations are believed to be the most stringent for data centers in Virginia.
objective: Impose 750-foot data center setbacks in Stafford County

summary: Amazon Web Services is building a $500M data center on 243 acres along Route 3 in Stevensburg, Culpeper County, with two buildings totaling 427,000 sq ft and approximately 90MW capacity. The project attracted opposition from the Coalition to Save Culpeper and the Piedmont Environmental Council. Culpeper's elected officials have approved over 12 million square feet of new data center campuses total. A special Town Council meeting in October 2025 discussed a Minimum Revenue Agreement between STACK Infrastructure and the Town of Culpeper. Opposition groups participated in Lobby Day in Richmond in January 2026 advocating for data center industry reform. Culpeper data centers are permitted to emit up to 55 dBA at night, which opponents say is ten times louder than WHO-recommended levels.
objective: Legal challenge to Amazon data center in Culpeper County

summary: Project Red Wolf is a $12B, 600MW data center campus by DCD Properties on 548 acres near Kansas Speedway, splitting 300MW north and 300MW south of Parallel Parkway. A protest petition and lawsuit by residents challenging the rezoning has put the project on hold. The Unified Government also has Project Yardbird (200MW conversion of former Quindaro Power Station; PowerTransitions has a $13.6M option) and Project Linda (200MW, location undisclosed) in the pipeline. As of early 2026, the lawsuit pending against Red Wolf remains the main obstacle; KCUR and The Beacon have published in-depth analyses of how data centers could raise BPU residential utility bills.
objective: Challenge TIF financing for Red Wolf DCD Properties data center

summary: Putnam County passed a one-year moratorium on wind, solar, data centers, and small modular nuclear reactor projects in November 2025. As of March 2026, commissioners are reviewing ordinances from White and Miami counties as references — including requirements for IDEM permits on backup generators, closed-loop or air-only cooling, and mandatory fire protection training for local fire departments. Putnam joins Marshall (Feb 2025), White (Oct 2025), and Fulton among Indiana counties enacting data center moratoria. Indiana state lawmakers took little action on data center bills in the 2026 session.
objective: Halt data centers and solar in Putnam County after Undisclosed proposal

summary: Wildcat Ridge proposed a $2.1 billion, 17.2M sq ft data center campus in Archbald. Borough Council approved a zoning amendment expanding data center eligibility from 4 to 9 districts. Residents filed a land use appeal in Lackawanna County Court, alleging the ordinance was drafted at developers' expense. Three data center developers intervened to defend the zoning. On March 18-19, Archbald Borough Council underwent a leadership coup driven by data center opposition — members voted to remove the president and VP. A 7th data center proposal (Project Green, Stavola Quarry) emerged.  Borough Council unanimously DENIED the 14-building data center campus on March 27, after a regime change ousted three council leaders on March 19.  Post-denial: Council member Aniska resigned. Denial was procedural (incomplete record) — developer could refile. The project involves the Wildcat Ridge Data Center Campus proposal, 1600 MW capacity, 574 acres.
objective: Legal challenge to Wildcat Ridge data center campus zoning expansion in Archbald

summary: Mesh Capital LLC sought to remove a protective proffer from a 57-acre property on Cabin Field Road. Supervisors voted 5-2 to deny the request in October 2025. However, since data centers have by-right use on heavy industrial zoned properties, the developer submitted three new site plans for a scaled-back 450,000 sq ft data center that staff determined to be in compliance with the proffer.
objective: Deny Mesh Capital data center on 57 acres in Campbell County

summary: Denver-based Amp Z proposed Project Lufkin, a multi-gigawatt data center on 1,000+ acres at the former Southland Paper Mill site near the Neches River in Angelina County, TX. Total investment estimated at $1.05-$1.5 billion. Phased buildout: 175MW grid-connected by 2026, 1.1GW by 2028, ultimately 2.1GW. Estimated 500 construction jobs over 4+ years and ~30 permanent positions. As of early 2026, the project remains under negotiation and has not been approved for construction; scope and layout still being developed. A Change.org petition voicing community concerns continues to circulate.
objective: Deny Amp Z 175MW data center permit in Lufkin

summary: Monarch Energy's proposed $12B, 800 MW data center on 1,100+ acres near Chicago-Rockford International Airport is advancing. Monarch Rock Air LLC was formed April 2025. On February 17, 2026, FERC accepted an agreement allowing Monarch Rock Air to use ComEd transmission lines. Construction could begin late 2026 or spring 2027, with early operations projected for 2028. The project would use up to 3 million gallons of water per day. Expected to create 1,000+ construction jobs and ~200 permanent technology jobs. Neighbors fear property destruction and environmental impacts.
objective: Petition to block Monarch Energy data center in Rockford

company: Oppidan Investment Co. / Longhorn Property Management / RR Realty Holdings
summary: Two data center developers are eyeing over 400 acres of undeveloped land in Foristell, a growing city along I-70 straddling St. Charles and Warren counties. A public hearing was delayed from Jan. 5 to Jan. 20, 2026 to find a larger venue. Resident Phyllis Elizabeth Wall brought a petition with 150+ signatures. The Change.org petition has 784 signatures. Residents cite concerns that Foristell lacks infrastructure and oversight for such development. UPDATE: The Foristell Board of Aldermen is scheduled to hold the second reading and final vote on the 216-acre land annexation on March 16, 2026. If approved, residents can file a protest petition within 14 days (requiring signatures of 5% of qualified voters). A Change.org petition has been launched. Developer: Oppidan Investment Company / Connect. The project involves the Oppidan Investment Co. proposal.
objective: 784-signature petition opposing Oppidan Investment Co. data center

opposition_groups: York County Citizens for Responsible Growth
summary: QTS is building a nine-building $1 billion campus near Lake Wylie, threatening the 95-acre Penland Christmas Tree Farm next door. Residents are concerned about noise, blinding security lights, and the sale of 400+ acres under the secretive code name 'Project COBRA' without community knowledge. Nearly 800 people signed a petition to stop the project. The tree farm owner worries increased heat could dry out his trees and ruin a nearly 60-year-old family business. Opposition group York County Citizens for Responsible Growth launched a Change.org petition with 900+ signatures. QTS held a face-to-face meeting with residents in February. York County Council has stood by the project and its 40-year incentive package. The project involves the QTS Data Centers proposal, $8B investment, 800 acres.
objective: Petition to block QTS Data Centers data center in York County

company: Whitmore & Blackwell (developer) / Indiana Economic Development Corporation
summary: A Change.org petition titled 'Prevent Data Centers from Establishing in Kokomo Indiana' gathered signatures citing concerns about water consumption, utility cost increases, and higher taxes. The Kokomo Common Council approved annexation and rezoning of 746 acres of formerly unincorporated Howard County land for industrial use, with Council Member Bob Stephenson the sole vote against both ordinances. Mayor Moore has publicly vouched to protect Kokomo from data centers, but more than a dozen residents shared concerns about the impacts of industrial development at the council meeting. The project involves the Whitmore & Blackwell proposal, 750 acres.
objective: Petition to block Whitmore & Blackwell data center in Kokomo

summary: South Strabane Township began drafting data center regulations after CNX Resources' 1,400-acre tract (with 400 buildable acres) was marketed by JLL for data center development. 'No Data Centers' signs appeared along roads. The township planning commission voted in March 2026 to recommend that the Board of Supervisors adopt a draft ordinance requiring 1,000-foot setbacks from residential homes and a community benefit agreement requiring developers to pay for police, fire, public safety, and infrastructure. Residents packed a 3-hour public hearing at the Oak Spring Road Fire Department in early April 2026. As of early April, CNX's property has not been sold, and there are no current plans to build a data center in the township. The Board of Supervisors is set to take up the ordinance at its April 28 meeting.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in South Strabane

opposition_groups: Cumberland County Residents Against Data Centers
summary: Energy Storage Solutions (ESS) is planning a 'NetZero' data center off Dunn Road in Fayetteville (Cumberland County, NC), built in 8 phases with $6.4B investment as part of ESS's $19.2B North Carolina footprint. The project would be powered by Piedmont Natural Gas and PWC (Fayetteville Utilities) 69kV line plus Duke Energy 115/230kV lines. As of early 2026, three of seven Cumberland County commissioners have publicly said they would vote for a moratorium on data centers following a spirited public hearing. No company is currently seeking formal approval; commissioners are positioning to get ahead of any application. Cumberland County is drafting its first data center rules. A Change.org petition continues to gather signatures opposing the project.
objective: Petition to block Energy Storage Solutions data center in Cumberland County

project_name: Tract Silver Springs / Multiple Data Centers
opposition_groups: Citizens Against Over Development in Lyon County
summary: Tract acquired 1,060 acres near Silver Springs in Lyon County to develop a datacenter park with up to 1.6GW capacity. The Lyon County Planning Commission unanimously approved the rezoning of 500+ acres. Some citizens expressed opposition citing environmental impacts and accusations of official corruption. Microsoft is also developing a 274-acre datacenter in Silver Springs. The first phase provides 150MW across 2-4 buildings. The project involves 1,060 acres.
objective: Block Tract rezoning of 1060 acres in Lyon County

summary: Takanock LLC proposed Project Baccara: 700 MW of natural gas generators and data center buildings on 160 acres near Surprise. A petition neared 5,000 signatures. The Arizona Corporation Commission approved a Certificate of Environmental Compatibility 5-0 in February 2026, but additional permits are still required.
objective: 5000-signature petition opposing Takanock data center

summary: Jonesborough enacted a 2-year moratorium on data processing centers and cryptocurrency operations, passing unanimously (Oct 13 first reading, Nov 3 final, 2025). Washington County followed in 2026, with commissioners voting 13-2 to approve a one-year moratorium on new data centers (including crypto mines) running through June 30, 2027. The Washington County resolution acknowledged data centers' 'extraordinary levels of electrical power' and grid strain. Separately, the Washington County Commission approved BWX Technologies' rezoning request for a 55-acre property at 1367 Old State Route 34 in Jonesborough, supporting BWXT's $714M expansion for a $1.5B federal contract for high-purity depleted uranium processing.
objective: Impose 2-year crypto mining moratorium in Jonesborough

summary: Norton City Council introduced a 180-day moratorium on data center zoning certificates after rejecting Quantum HPC's proposed 485,000 sq ft data center ('Project Triton') at the former PPG limestone mine. On October 20, council voted 5-0 to block the project after the company's founder was found to have made false statements about his education and litigation history. Quantum HPC withdrew their proposal. The project involves 90 acres.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after Quantum HPC proposal

summary: Wiscasset Selectboard voted in November 2025 to pause conversations about a $5B data center on town-owned land along the Back River after resident Richard Davis launched 'Protect Wiscasset,' a grassroots campaign. Little information about the end user, utility usage, or power source had been disclosed after the project was first revealed on September 16, 2025, alarming residents. The selectboard is developing a transparent evaluation process with no established timeline for completion. The project involves 300 acres.
objective: Oppose Undisclosed (under NDA) data center in Wiscasset

opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development
summary: Tract received approval for a 708MW hyperscale data center consisting of 12 buildings up to 80 feet tall and 2.53 million sq ft, plus an electrical substation and several hundred generators with over a million gallons of stored fuel. Residents filed a lawsuit against the City of Farmington, alleging environmental and public health harms including air quality degradation, noise, and strain on water and electricity supplies. The Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development was formed to coordinate opposition. Castle Rock Township told Farmington and the Metropolitan Council it didn't consent to the proposed zoning changes, just weeks before the city council approved the project. Tract requested a one-year extension of the plat recording deadline to May 2026. The lawsuit is in discovery with a trial potentially in summer or fall 2026. The project involves $5B investment, 330 acres.
objective: Legal challenge to Tract data center in Farmington

summary: Johnson County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on data center rezoning and permits in unincorporated areas on Nov. 8, 2025. The moratorium runs through November 8, 2026. The county is 3-4 months from finalizing a comprehensive ordinance addressing location, water usage, and facility size. While no firms had proposed building in Johnson County, the moratorium was adopted proactively given the regional surge in data center proposals.
objective: Impose one-year moratorium on data center rezoning and permits in unincorporated Johnson County

summary: Tama County Board of Supervisors approved moratoriums on both data mining/centers and battery storage on November 10, 2025. Applies to all proposed facilities regardless of size, technology, or location. Typo corrections made in follow-up meetings Nov 17-18. Key concerns driving this action include zoning concerns. The outcome remains pending.
objective: Pause data centers in Tama County pending regulations

summary: Barberton City Council approved an indefinite moratorium on data centers in November 2025. In neighboring Norton, Quantum HPC's Project Triton proposal for the old PPG mine on Limestone Drive was killed in October 2025 after a contentious town hall meeting on October 13, where the Quantum HPC founder made statements the city law director determined were not true. The developer informed the city the representative was no longer working for them, and the project was withdrawn. Around 18 Ohio municipalities have enacted or are considering data center moratoriums.
objective: Impose indefinite data centers moratorium after Quantum HPC proposal

summary: Mitchell County approved moratoriums on utility-scale solar (Resolution #1326-25, Nov 12, 2025, unanimous) and data centers until Dec 31, 2026 (Resolution #1336-25, Nov 25, 2025, 4-1 vote). Supervisor Wherry was the key proponent. The county also has ongoing disputes about the Salt Creek Wind project.
objective: Pause data centers and solar in Mitchell County pending regulations

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Hamilton City Council Chambers were packed on Nov. 12 with residents voicing concerns about Logistix's proposed Hamilton Innovation Center, a 240 MW, 320,000 sq ft data center on 29 acres. Resident Teri Cunningham created the Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio Facebook group with 600+ members. Citizens circulate petitions, create yard signs, and speak at biweekly council meetings. The proposal remains under review. The project involves $1B investment.
objective: Petition to block Logistix data center in Hamilton Township

summary: TAC Data Centers pulled out of its option to lease 569 acres adjacent to the AgriTech Park in Great Falls, Montana in November 2025, ending a $1-1.5 billion, 2-million-square-foot, 600 MW campus plan known as Project Cardinal. The project would have demanded up to 600 MW by 2030 — approximately 88% of what Montana's existing NorthWestern Energy customers use. TAC's subsidiary Ardent Tech said it could not proceed without NorthWestern Energy's cooperation in securing sufficient power supply.
objective: TAC Data Centers withdraws from 569-acre Great Falls project

opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
summary: Nine Montana nonprofits filed a PSC complaint to stop NorthWestern Energy from shifting data center infrastructure costs to 413,000+ residential customers. NWE signed Letters of Intent with 11 data center developers for up to 1,400 MW without regulatory approval. In March 2026, Earthjustice challenged a PSC protective order shielding LOI details, arguing NWE hasn't shown trade secret qualification. The complaint is ongoing. The project involves 5,000 acres.
objective: File PSC complaint over NorthWestern shifting costs to residents

company: Atlas Power / Sabey Data Center Properties
opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
summary: Sabey Data Centers proposed two 500,000 sq ft buildings (1M sq ft total) on over 600 acres at Montana Connections Industrial Park in Butte, MT (Silver Bow County), requiring 325 MW of power and water-based cooling. Atlas Power's existing Butte facility (former Mike Mansfield Advanced Tech Center, formerly CryptoWatt) operates at 75 MW air-cooled and is in 'contractual estimate' stage with NorthWestern Energy for 75 MW more by 2026, growing another 75 MW over the subsequent 3-5 years. Atlas plans to expand its flagship operation. NorthWestern is reportedly in talks with 11 separate data center developers as of February 2026, raising concerns about water impacts and ratepayer cost shifts.
objective: Organize coalition against Atlas Power data center in Butte

summary: Pittsfield Township enacted a 180-day data center moratorium in November 2025 — one of at least 27 Michigan communities to do so. Township representative Morgan Foreman is co-sponsoring the bipartisan three-bill data center moratorium package introduced by Michigan lawmakers in late February 2026 (Reps. Wortz, Fox, and Wegela). The 'Data Center Regulation Act' would prohibit new data centers from receiving state or local permits or commencing operations until April 1, 2027. However, the moratorium legislation faces uncertain prospects under Speaker Matt Hall and Gov. Gretchen Whitmer.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Pittsfield Township

summary: Sullivan County Commissioner Jessica Means introduced a 4-month moratorium resolution on data mining and crypto mining centers on November 20, 2025. The commission deferred the vote at their December meeting. Subsequently, a major data center project was announced for Sullivan County (Indiana, near Merom Generating Station / Heartland Industrial Park, 1,000+ acres). Phase 1: $20B investment, 750 peak construction jobs, 125 permanent jobs. Phase 2: $45B, 2,000 peak construction jobs, 375 permanent. Project includes seven buildings and two end users. Community benefit package includes $25M to the county per phase plus $4.5M for local programs (Brownbaggers School Food, Merom Camp, Sullivan County Park and Lake).
objective: Impose 4-month crypto mining moratorium in Sullivan County

opposition_groups: Stop the Data Centers Livingston County
Livingston County Residents for Responsible Development
summary: Township enacted a unanimous six-month moratorium on Nov. 20, 2025 after ~2 hours of public comment. Trustee Bob Wilson confirmed Meta was behind the project, estimating a $9B investment and the center occupying at least 20% of the 1,000-acre space. The group 'Stop the Data Centers Livingston County' said the moratorium as passed did not apply to the current developer. In December 2025, the developers withdrew the rezoning request, stating they wanted to 'honor the current moratorium.' FracTracker Alliance celebrated the withdrawal as a community win. Developer withdrew $1B project after resident outcry, but moratorium remains in effect while township drafts zoning rules. The project involves 1,000 acres.
objective: Halt data centers in Howell Township after Meta proposal

opposition_groups: Vicksburg Residents Against Data Center (ad hoc)
summary: Amazon/AWS announced a $3 billion data center campus near the Port of Vicksburg on Nov 20, 2025, after 2.5 years of secret development behind NDAs. Community forums revealed widespread concern about transparency, water usage, and environmental impacts. The project was named one of 2025's 'Worst Economic Development Deals.' Opposition is ongoing. The project involves 175 acres.
objective: Oppose Amazon data center over water in Vicksburg

summary: Marshall County became the first Indiana county to pass a data center moratorium in early February 2025. Commissioner and zoning board member Stan Klotz pushed for the pause, citing concerns about development impacts observed in other areas. Following Marshall County's lead, White County adopted a moratorium in October 2025 and Putnam County in November 2025, making three Indiana counties with active moratoriums. The moratorium is intended to give leaders time to better understand long-term impacts of large data facilities and update zoning rules if necessary.
objective: Halt data centers in Marshall County after Undisclosed proposal

summary: Serverfarm acquired 135 acres through subsidiary SF ARK1 LLC, north of the water treatment plant on Palmer Road in Clarksville, Arkansas. Plans call for six buildings totaling over 2 million sq ft with 318 MW capacity. The project could cost $8 billion at full build-out. Residents are calling for a pause and public hearing, stating 'Arkansas land, water, and communities are not for sale.' Despite a Change.org petition, the city has approved the project.
objective: Petition to block Serverfarm data center in Clarksville

summary: Amazon/AWS proposed a $4B datacenter on 471 acres in Wilmington with a 30-year, 100% property tax abatement (effective 70% abatement). Jessica Sharp organized the Facebook group 'Wilmington Residents for Responsible Development' which grew to nearly 3,000 members. WRRD demands Tier 4 EPA generators, air quality monitoring, closed-loop cooling, and a Community Benefits Agreement. Daily water demand could exceed 1 million gallons. Retired executive Kurt Cooper serves as liaison to officials. Wilmington City Council approved annexation and rezoning of ~545 acres for AWS despite continued opposition. However, separate site-plan, utility, and tax-incentive reviews are still required before construction. The site plan was tabled after city staff identified missing details on water service demands, cooling methods, noise mitigation, and pollution -- which AWS called 'proprietary information.' Wilmington Council voted 5-2 on Feb 20 to rezone 545 acres (4 parcels) from rural residential to light industrial, clearing a major procedural hurdle for AWS. A second data center by Ardent Technologies (affiliated with TAC Data Centers) got an additional 500 acres rezoned for a $6B investment. Planning Commission tabled a conditional use amendment 5-1 on March 3. WRRD (Wilmington Residents for Responsible Development) named Kurt Cooper as liaison, pushing for moratorium and planning legal challenge. The project involves 1500 MW capacity.
objective: Require use permit for Amazon/AWS data center in Wilmington

summary: Hogansville City Council approved a 90-day moratorium halting all data center applications, including Fertile Ground Investments' proposal for a 600 MW data center on 437 acres of the city's old sprayfield property on Hightower Road. The developer had until March 2026 to present a proposal contingent on rezoning from residential to industrial. The project would bring ~$400K/year in tax revenue but only 30-35 jobs. Residents expressed concerns about property values, water consumption, energy costs, and utility bill increases.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after Fertile Ground Investments proposal

summary: Monroe County passed a data center moratorium on unincorporated land after commissioners unanimously rejected the 900-acre, $5.8 billion Bolingbroke Technology Campus. Nearly 900 residents packed the county meeting to challenge the project, citing traffic, water use, and industrialization of rural land. The property owner subsequently sued the county. The project involves the Otis Ingram proposal, 1100 MW capacity.
objective: Impose moratorium on data centers in Monroe County after rejecting $5.8B Bolingbroke campus

summary: Athens-Clarke County Commission is voting March 3 on whether to extend the current emergency moratorium (set to expire March 6). Commissioners are discussing regulations including closed-loop cooling requirements, on-site clean energy for facilities over 5MW, and potentially an outright ban on data centers. Commissioner Tiffany Taylor supports an outright ban and banning NDAs for data center proposals. Environmental groups released formal recommendations for data center rules in February. Moratorium extended unanimously to June 5, 2026 at March 3 meeting. Commission voted to send text amendment proposals back to planning commission for revision. Considering Level 3 industrial threshold requirement (special use permit) for all data centers. UPDATE: Athens-Clarke County Commission voted unanimously on March 11 to extend its data center moratorium to June 5, 2026 (originally set to expire March 6). One proposed regulation would categorize data centers as Level 3 industrial, requiring a special use permit. Commissioner Melissa Link: 'We need to make it as onerous as possible.' The project involves the FAE Clark LLC proposal, 233 acres.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after FAE Clark (end-user unknown) proposal

summary: Cloverleaf Infrastructure scrapped plans in Carlton (Kewaunee County) after local residents objected to the idea. Town Chairman David Hardtke told Cloverleaf 'if this is such a good thing for the town I want the public informational meeting and they would not do that -- it's not good for the town.' Chris Kohnle of Tisch Mills Farm Center warned 'once they take land away, it'll never come back.' The developer was subsequently also rejected in the Village of Greenleaf (Brown County). Carlton's proximity to Lake Michigan made it attractive for cooling.
objective: Force Cloverleaf Infrastructure to scrap data center plans in Carlton after public opposition

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
summary: Big Rapids Township passed an ordinance banning data centers in its 120-acre airport industrial park, part of a wave of at least 27 Michigan communities enacting moratoria or zoning restrictions on data center development amid concerns about grid strain and inadequate local zoning codes. Opposition was led by Michiganders Against Data Centers and Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan. A petition gathered 11,077 signatures. The outcome remains pending.
objective: Ban data centers in Big Rapids Township's airport industrial park

opposition_groups: Stop the Data Center on Taylor Lane (Facebook group)
Stop the Data Center on Taylor Lane
summary: Infrakey, a data center company formed in March 2025, proposed a $10B, 925 MW, 21-building data center campus on 520 acres west of Elm Mott in Lacy Lakeview (McLennan County, TX) — the largest industrial development in McLennan County history. Includes a dedicated 1.2 GW behind-the-meter natural gas power plant and reclaimed water cooling. Lacy Lakeview signed an MOU with Infrakey in June 2025. Project would generate $50M/year in tax revenue for the 7,000-resident town. As of early 2026 the project remains in 'planning' stage with a 4-5 year buildout. Concerns from neighboring small towns who say they 'knew nothing about it.'
objective: Petition to block Infrakey data center in McLennan County

opposition_groups: Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability
summary: Energy Storage Solutions (ESS) plans a $6.4B, 900 MW, 300-acre data center and energy storage campus at the Kingsboro Business Park (just west of the former QVC plant near U.S. 64 / Kingsboro Road), Tarboro, NC — part of ESS's $19.2B North Carolina footprint. ESS expected to break ground Q1 2026. Built behind-the-meter using natural gas 'green' generation and a BESS storage system, in 8 phases. Would add $75M/year in tax revenue for Edgecombe County. The data center could use up to 5 million gallons/day in operations while Edgecombe County faces severe drought conditions. Significant community opposition, including riverkeepers, NCEJN, and Sound Rivers. An Edgecombe data center advisory group has formed.
objective: Oppose Energy Storage Solutions data center over water in Edgecombe County

project_name: Project Matador / Trump Advanced Energy and Intelligence Campus
summary: Project Matador / Trump Advanced Energy and Intelligence Campus, proposed by Fermi America on approximately 5,236 acres 12 miles NE of Amarillo, received a 5.1 GW air permit from TCEQ. The air permits would allow 93 gas-fired turbines around the perimeter, making it the largest gas plant in the US if built. Authorized to release approximately 5,400 tons/year of regulated air pollutants (NOx, CO, VOCs, particulate matter) and up to 23.6 million tons/year of greenhouse gases. Over 279 comments were filed with TCEQ (mostly voicing concerns about) plus 63 contested case hearing requests. The facility would consume 2.5-5.5 million gallons/day from municipal water sources threatening the Ogallala Aquifer, with some residents estimating only five more years of water remaining. Construction paused pending TCEQ permits; a key tenant terminated its agreement in December 2025. The project involves 7650 MW capacity, 5,236 acres.
objective: Challenge Fermi America's 7.65 GW air permit for 93 gas-fired turbines near Amarillo

summary: Sabey Data Centers proposed a $4B, 130-acre, 250MW campus on the southwest side of Indianapolis in Decatur Township. The Decatur Township Civic Council's executive committee unanimously voted against it. About 150 residents attended public meetings voicing concerns about the project, citing concerns about noise, energy demand, water use, lack of jobs, and impacts on property values. Land use committee chair Pat Andrews stated: 'It does not belong in the middle of these residences.' Sabey filed only variance requests rather than a full rezoning petition, which prevented the city-county councilor from calling down the vote to the full council. On March 18, 2026, the Metropolitan Development Commission approved the project. Sabey committed over $5 million for road improvements, closed-loop water systems, and a ban on cryptocurrency mining at the site.
objective: Rally against Sabey Data Centers data center in Decatur Township

opposition_groups: Kenoshans Unite Against Microsoft Data Center
summary: Microsoft purchased 240 acres in Kenosha County near I-94 and Route 142 in early 2025, with the data center scheduled to open in early 2026. The city signed an NDA with Microsoft in May 2024, keeping the project secret for six months. A resident's viral TikTok video triggered a wave of community opposition. Kenosha City Hall was standing-room only on Nov. 17 as residents packed Common Council chambers. Citizens called data centers 'parasites' and 'terrible neighbors.' A Change.org petition was created to stop the project. The project involves 450 MW capacity, $4B investment.
objective: Petition to block Microsoft data center in Kenosha

summary: Janesville signed an NDA with Viridian Acquisitions LLC for a proposed $8B, 800 MW data center on 250 acres, sparking resident opposition. The group SNOW (Stop Needless Opposition Waste) determined the data center would not reliably improve quality of life. Protests were held in Janesville as part of statewide Wisconsin opposition to data centers. Community concerns center on secrecy deals and economic claims.
objective: Rally against Viridian Acquisitions data center in Janesville

opposition_groups: Beaver Dam Education on Data Center Group
summary: Meta's $1B Beaver Dam data center campus (700,000+ sq ft, optimized for AI workloads) could use up to 220 MW of power, per newly unredacted portions of a proposed Alliant Energy service contract revealed March 2026. Will support ~1,000 construction jobs and 100+ operational jobs. The campus uses dry-cooling tech, eliminating operational water demand for cooling, and Meta committed to restoring 100% of consumed water to local watersheds. Initial data center expected online in 2027. Meta plans a Data Center Community Action Grants program launching fall 2026. Separately, a second smaller data center proposal from Oppidan (90,000 sq ft on 13 city-owned acres) is now also under consideration in Beaver Dam.
objective: Sue for environmental review of Meta project

summary: Gemini Capital proposed a $1.9B, 500 MW hyperscale data center on 164 acres in eastern Sioux Falls. The City Council voted 7-0 on Jan. 6, 2026 to rezone the farmland for light industrial use, despite 200+ people attending in opposition with over 5 hours of mostly negative testimony. Opponents Samantha Scarlata and Amanda Kendall formed 'Let Sioux Falls Vote' and circulated referendum petitions, but fell short with 5,012 signatures submitted vs. the required 6,704 (5% of registered voters).
objective: Force public referendum on Gemini Capital data center in Sioux Falls

project_name: Strata Expanse AI Center of Excellence
opposition_groups: Stop AI Data Centers in Lawrence County
summary: Houston-based Strata Expanse hosted a ribbon cutting for an AI data center in the Haverhill area at 1476 County Road 1A. A petition gathered 5,178 signatures voicing concerns about the project. The nearby city of Ironton passed a data center moratorium on first reading. Community members demand environmental and community impact assessments before construction proceeds. The project involves 80 MW capacity.
objective: Halt data centers in Lawrence County after Strata Expanse proposal

company: City of Newport News (Economic Development Authority)
project_name: Potential Data Center near Fort Eustis
opposition_groups: Newport News Residents Against Data Centers
summary: A resident created a petition after word spread about a potential data center near Fort Eustis, gathering 3,500+ signatures. Residents rallied against the proposal. However, city officials state there are no conversations, plans, or negotiations underway to bring a data center to Newport News. The petition demonstrates pre-emptive community opposition. The project involves the City of Newport News proposal, 20 acres.
objective: Petition to block City of Newport News data center in Newport News

summary: Moorestown Township Council opted out of data center development on a 25-acre vacant site (former Pulverizer Services / New Albany Business Park Redevelopment Area) along New Albany Road. The township issued a public RFQ/RFP in 2024 seeking a redeveloper, but Council members opted not to move forward with data center proposals. The township plans to re-engage in the RFQ/RFP process in 2026 to explore other uses serving the long-term interests of residents. A New Jersey Policy Perspective report titled 'Fool's Gold: The Hidden Costs of AI Data Centers for New Jersey' has bolstered statewide opposition arguments.
objective: Deny New Urban data center on 25 acres in Moorestown

summary: Monroe Township Council introduced Ordinance O:20-2026 on March 25, 2026 to amend the Hexa Builders Redevelopment Plan, eliminating data centers and all related uses from the permitted uses list. This reverses the council's earlier Ordinance O:02-2025 from June 2025 that had added data centers as permitted. The action came after residents packed the March 11 council meeting in opposition when it was revealed the 1.6 million sq ft Hexa redevelopment on Black Horse Pike could include an AI data center. Mayor Wolfe announced the data center would not come to town. The final vote on the ban ordinance is scheduled for April 8, 2026. The mayor's office was also researching a possible broader data center ban ordinance.
objective: Remove data centers from Hexa Builders Redevelopment Plan permitted uses in Monroe Township

summary: Breckinridge County Fiscal Court passed an AI Data Center Moratorium ordinance in November 2025. Meanwhile, TeraWulf is advancing a $3-4B large-scale data center campus in nearby Hawesville (Hancock County) with access to 480 MW of grid-connected power. Fluor signed a Limited Notice to Proceed for preconstruction services in early 2026. Kentucky is seeing a wave of data center interest, with a GOP bill in 2026 seeking guardrails to ensure new data centers 'pay their own way.'
objective: Pause data centers and solar in Breckinridge County pending regulations

summary: Tyrone Township (Livingston County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development on December 2, 2025. The township is located near Howell Township, where a major $1 billion Meta data center proposal generated significant community opposition. Opposition was led by Michiganders Against Data Centers. A petition gathered 11,077 signatures. Key concerns driving this action include zoning concerns. The outcome remains pending.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Tyrone Township

summary: 4,500-acre, $300M solar farm near Stockton would power a Meta data center. 'Stop Solar in Stockton' Facebook group gained 2,000+ members. State Sen. Albritton filed two bills: one-year statewide moratorium on solar farm construction, and another giving county commissions regulation authority. Baldwin County hired Jacobs Engineering for third-party environmental review. The project involves 4,500 acres.
objective: Oppose Meta data center in Baldwin County

opposition_groups: Starke County - No Data Center IN
summary: Starke County Planning Commission unanimously advanced a one-year moratorium on hyperscale data centers exceeding 5,000 sq ft, making it the fourth Indiana county to act. The moratorium was triggered by leaked emails from Spain-based ABEI Energy inquiring about rezoning agricultural parcels for a data center employing 4,000-6,000 people.
objective: Halt data centers in Starke County after ABEI Energy proposal

summary: Imperial County is finalizing approval of an approximately 950,000 sq ft, 330MW data center plus 330MW emergency backup generators and 862MWh energy storage system — a $10B project intended to power AI development for one of the country's largest tech companies. The Imperial County Board of Supervisors is scheduled to vote on the project's appealed lot merger on April 7, 2026. The facility could consume nearly double the entirety of Imperial County's 2024 electricity use and need 750,000 gallons of water per day. The developer is openly seeking to bypass California's environmental review (CEQA) process. Residents are gathering signatures for a November 2026 ballot initiative — the 'Imperial County Data Center Prohibition Act' — to block all data centers in the county. 'No Data Center' chants rang out at the county board meeting in late March 2026. Sierra Club San Gorgonio chapter is leading opposition.
objective: Legal challenge to Imperial Valley Computer data center in Imperial County

summary: Endeavour Energy's 560-acre, 3M sq ft, four-building data center campus south of Meta's facility. Standing-room-only public hearing; petition against the project circulated. Despite opposition, DeKalb City Council voted unanimously to approve Dec 8, 2025. Opposition was led by Stop Project Vector (ad hoc). A petition gathered 821 signatures. Key concerns driving this action include community impacts.
objective: Petition to block Endeavour Energy data center in DeKalb

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
summary: Green Charter Township adopted a one-year moratorium on data center development on Dec. 9, 2025, citing concerns about water consumption, energy usage, and noise. The township was among the earliest of what became at least 27 Michigan communities to enact data center moratoria, part of a statewide backlash that escalated to bipartisan legislation (HB 5594-5596) proposing a statewide construction halt until April 2027.
objective: Halt data centers in Green Charter after Undisclosed proposal

summary: St. Joseph County Council rejected Amazon/AWS's $13B datacenter rezoning 7-2 after a marathon 10-hour meeting, following an earlier unanimous unfavorable recommendation from the Area Plan Commission. Citizens for Responsible Land Use led the opposition. The proposal would have required 2,250 MW of power. The council cited concerns about water usage, grid strain, and lack of community benefit as reasons for the denial. The project involves 2250 MW capacity, 1,200 acres.
objective: Deny Amazon/AWS rezoning of 1200 acres of farmland

opposition_groups: Florida Native Plant Society PBC Chapter
Stop the Palm Beach DC Project Tango
summary: Project Tango proposes the first hyperscale data center in Palm Beach County on 202 acres at the Central Park Commerce Center in Loxahatchee. The project has been scaled back to 1 million square feet from its original proposal. Palm Beach County commissioners voted 7-0 to postpone the zoning hearing to April 23, 2026 to allow for further environmental and traffic studies. Residents and environmental groups raised concerns about water usage, noise, and impacts to the Loxahatchee Slough. The project involves the PBA Holdings proposal, $10B investment.
objective: Deny PBA Holdings / TPA Group data center on 202 acres in Palm Beach County

summary: AV Squared, backed by Chamath Palihapitiya, proposed a $25B, 1.5 GW AI data center campus on 2,000 acres near Tonopah, Arizona. The Maricopa County Board unanimously approved industrial rezoning in December 2025 despite ~200 public comments in opposition. The facility would use 3.6 billion gallons of water annually. Residents raised concerns about impacts to groundwater, traffic, and the rural desert landscape. The project involves 2,000 acres.
objective: Petition to block AV Squared data center in Tonopah

summary: City Council voted 7-0 on December 12, 2025 to reject Active Infrastructure's $2.5B, 150 MW AI data center proposal on 40 acres near Queen Creek and Dobson roads. The city received over 250 emails in opposition and only 10 in support. City planning staff said the proposal did not meet Chandler's vision for the South Price Road Corridor, which prioritizes knowledge-based industries. Former U.S. Sen. Kyrsten Sinema appeared before the planning commission to advocate for the project. Chandler was among the first municipalities to restrict data center development in 2016.
objective: Deny Active Infrastructure 150MW data center permit in City of Chandler

opposition_groups: STOP Project Sail Newnan Data Center
summary: Coweta County's moratorium on data center applications expired December 17, 2025, and the Board of Commissioners adopted a data center ordinance on December 16, 2025 in a 4-1 vote. The ordinance allows data centers in light industrial and industrial zones with strict requirements on location, buffering, design, noise, lighting, traffic, and utility capacity. Chairman Bill McKenzie voted against, citing concerns about building height (70 ft with rooftop equipment) and lack of a decommissioning mandate. Prologis/Atlas Development's Project Sail (600-900MW, 831 acres) is expected to file for rezoning in early 2026.
objective: Oppose 900MW Prologis data center on 832 acres in Coweta County

summary: DeKalb County, Georgia Commission extended its data center moratorium until June 23, 2026 at the December 16, 2025 meeting due to community concerns about public health and environmental impacts. A public hearing on a proposed comprehensive data center ordinance is scheduled for May 12, 2026.
