summary: After Natelli Investments withdrew the New Hill Digital Campus application on March 5, the Apex Town Council voted unanimously to move forward with a proposed one-year moratorium on data center development. A public hearing is scheduled for April 14, 2026 at 6 p.m. at Apex Town Hall. Mayor Jacques Gilbert also proposed a non-standing committee to research data center impacts.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Town of Apex

summary: In Hanover County, Virginia, Developer Tract proposed the Mountain Road Tech Park — a 427-acre, 900-MW data center campus along Route 33 and Mountain Road near the Hanover-Henrico line (430 acres). The project would include five buildings, requiring up to 600,000 gallons/day of water. The Planning Commission unanimously deferred the proposal. It is being reconsidered at the March 19, 2026 meeting. Planning Commission deferred again on March 19 (second deferral). Rescheduled to April 16.
objective: Rally against Tract data center in Hanover County

summary: In Wise County, Virginia, Red Post Energy Group signed a letter of intent with Wise, Virginia, Innovation Hub Venture (OASIS) to develop the Maverick Project — a phased natural gas power plant for a data center complex adjacent to Lonesome Pine Regional Industrial Facility (300 acres). The project comprises nine buildings totaling 2 million sq ft with 600 MW capacity and an on-site gas power plant. Appalachian Voices opposes the project. The complex would create 500-700 construction jobs and 30-50 operations jobs per building.
objective: Oppose Red Post Energy Group data center in Wise County

summary: The Moore County, Tennessee, Metro Council is considering a two-year moratorium on data center construction and expansion, which would take effect April 20, 2026. During the moratorium, the county would stop accepting new applications for special use permits, zoning amendments, and building permits related to data centers. County staff would study potential effects on energy use, water demand, noise, and land use in coordination with local utility providers.
objective: Pause data centers in Moore County pending regulations

summary: Three citizen groups -- Tucker United, West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club -- filed an appeal challenging the state Air Quality Board's decision to uphold an air quality permit for the proposed Ridgeline power plant and data center in Tucker County, located between Davis and Thomas (proposed by Fundamental Data LLC). The 500-acre facility would include a natural gas power plant fueling a data center. The appeal argues the WVDEP wrongly classified the facility as a 'synthetic minor source' rather than a 'major source' of emissions.
objective: Challenge Fundamental Data air quality permits in court

bill_name: H.727 - Vermont Sustainable Data Centers Act
summary: Vermont H.727, the Sustainable Data Centers Act, sponsored by Rep. Laura Sibilia (I-Dover), regulates 'factory-sized developments' requiring 20+ MW power capacity. Focuses on proper siting, environmental protections, and ratepayer cost controls but applies the same requirements to all data centers regardless of technology or environmental footprint. A companion Senate bill, S.205, would ban data centers over 100 MW until July 2030 pending a Public Utility Commission study.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in VT

summary: Rhode Island S2346 establishes 30-year property, tangible, sales and use tax exemptions for qualified data centers. Proposed by Sen. DiPalma (D), who also works as a technical director at Raytheon. Critics argued tech companies would build data centers without incentives; supporters noted 42 other states already offer sales-tax exemptions. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10 after nearly an hour of floor debate.
objective: Establish 30-year property and sales tax exemptions for data centers in Rhode Island

summary: Rhode Island S2776 requires data centers needing large amounts of electricity to fund the cost of necessary infrastructure improvements to supply that power, rather than passing costs to existing ratepayers. Rhode Island currently hosts seven data centers, none of which would meet the proposed threshold. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10, 2026, alongside companion tax incentive bill S2346.
objective: Require data centers to fund electrical infrastructure upgrades rather than shift costs to ratepayers in RI

bill_name: LD 713 - Exclude Data Centers from Tax Exemptions
summary: Maine LD 713 would exclude data centers from Maine's Business Equipment Tax Exemption (BETE) program (100% property tax break) and the Dirigo Business Incentives Programs. The bill also requires the Maine DECD to study other state incentives data centers may benefit from. Supported by the Natural Resources Council of Maine, the bill was presented to the Taxation Committee on March 12, 2026. The bill is pending committee action.
objective: Exclude data centers from Maine business tax exemption programs

summary: Sentinel Data Centers has an agreement to occupy 1 million square feet on two floors of a former paper mill in Jay, Maine, employing 800-1,000 construction workers and 125 permanent staff. The project faces an existential threat from LD 307, a bill advancing in the Maine Legislature that would impose a two-year moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Notably, the developer has not encountered local opposition from Jay residents, partly because the project does not seek special tax breaks.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Sentinel Data Centers in Jay

opposition_groups: Sanford Residents Concerned About Data Centers
summary: Northern New England Energy Company is proposing the 'Sanford Woods Industrial and Technical Campus,' a 100-300MW data center covering over 1,000 acres along the Mousam River in Sanford. The developer claims the facility would generate its own power using fuel cells, solar, and battery storage without burdening Maine's grid. The project is threatened by LD 307, a proposed state moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Local residents have raised concerns about noise, environmental damage, and the significant changes a facility of this scale would bring.
objective: Oppose Northern New England Energy data center over noise in Sanford

summary: Connecticut legislators introduced a bill that would codify a 'bring your own power' mandate effective July 1, 2026. Under the proposal, generators supplying electricity to large customers (20+ MW) via private connection must obtain a PURA license and produce 100% of projected demand plus reserve margin using new generation. Large customers must post financial guarantees covering 85% of requested service for 10+ years. The bill is pending committee action.
objective: Codify bring-your-own-power mandate for data centers in Connecticut
sponsors: Joint Committee on Energy and Technology

summary: A massive estimated $10 billion data center project backed by Georgia Lt. Gov. Burt Jones and his family is proposed for Butts County as part of the Highway 16 Interstate Health Development near I-75, which would include 11 million square feet of data centers. The project is expected to consume over 4.5 million gallons of water per day, more than tripling Butts County's current water usage. Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr accused Jones of using his office to benefit his family's property interests, and environmental advocates have flagged the lack of available information about the facility's cooling technology and energy demands.
objective: Oppose $10B data center backed by Lt. Gov. Jones over water strain in Butts County

bill_name: SB 265 - Data Center Tax Incentive Reform
summary: Alabama SB265, sponsored by Sen. Andrew Jones (R-Centre), limits data center tax abatement periods to 20 years starting January 1, 2027, down from the current 30-year maximum. The bill also mandates that large-scale data centers consuming 100+ MW pay state sales and use taxes. The governor may waive taxes for economically struggling counties. Passed the Senate 30-0 on March 13, 2026, with Governor Kay Ivey's support as part of a broader package aimed at lowering costs for Alabama families.
objective: Limit data center tax abatements to 20 years in Alabama

summary: In Alabama, SB 354 would impose a one-year moratorium on construction of new large-scale solar power facilities, Prompted by opposition to a 4,500-acre solar project in Stockton meant to power Meta's Montgomery data center expansion. Exempts TVA solar capacity and projects already under construction. Opposition was led by Stockton Area Residents Against Solar. Key concerns driving this action include environmental impacts and loss of farmland. The outcome remains pending.
objective: Impose one-year moratorium on large-scale solar facilities tied to data center expansion in Alabama

summary: Following Governor Jeff Landry's announcement of Amazon's $12 billion investment in three interconnected data center campuses across Caddo, Louisiana, and Bossier parishes, Commissioner Chris Kracman proposed a one-year moratorium on developments using water from Caddo Lake, but the resolution failed to receive a second from any other commissioner. Amazon has pledged to limit cooling water use to less than 13% of the year and invest $400 million in public water systems in the Caddo-Bossier area.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Caddo Parish

opposition_groups: Benton Area Residents Concerned About Data Centers
summary: As part of Amazon's $12 billion Northwest Louisiana data center investment, STACK Infrastructure is developing a 1,089-acre 'Highway 3 Technology Park' north of Benton in Bossier Parish, planned for over 1.3 million square feet across six buildings. The Bossier Parish Police Jury scheduled a public hearing for March 18, 2026 -- the first opportunity for public comment since the project was confirmed. Residents have raised concerns about air quality, proximity to homes, water supply, and rising utility costs.
objective: Challenge STACK Infrastructure 1,089-acre data center in Bossier Parish

summary: Florida SB 1118 creates a public records exemption allowing local governments to keep data center siting plans confidential for up to 12 months when requested by the company. A developer's 'proprietary confidential business information' remains exempt until it becomes public or the company stops treating it as proprietary. Includes a 5-year sunset provision expiring October 2031. The bill was enacted alongside companion SB 484, which establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers effective July 1, 2026.
objective: Create public records exemption shielding data center siting plans for 12 months (SB 1118)

opposition_groups: Stop the Monticello Data Centers MN
summary: UPDATE: Monticello residents rallied against two proposed data centers (600+ acres combined, 4M+ sq ft). An online petition exceeded 1,200 signatures. Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy filed a lawsuit against the city challenging the review process. A Facebook opposition group quickly gained traction. Residents gathered at local parks to distribute yard signs.
objective: Legal challenge to Monticello Tech data center in Monticello

summary: CloudHQ's 1.4M sq ft data center at West Creek Corporate Center received preliminary approval from the Chaska, Minnesota, City Council on October 21, 2024, with a 4-1 vote. The lone dissenter was Ward 2 representative Taylor Hubbard. Residents voiced concerns about the 72.5-foot building height (double the 36-foot limit), noise, and neighborhood character impacts. Planning Commission raised issues about unprecedented height and landscaping requirements. Final site plan approval is pending future Planning Commission and City Council review.
objective: Oppose CloudHQ data center over noise in Chaska

summary: The Warrenton, Missouri, Board of Aldermen voted unanimously in January 2026 to approve a conditional use permit for a Crusoe data center on a 340-acre parcel, consisting of two 805,000-square-foot buildings and a 40,000-square-foot administration building. More than 40 residents spoke both for and against the proposal, with opponents raising concerns about noise, light pollution, water supply impacts on aquifers, and the pace of the approval process.
objective: Approve conditional use permit for Crusoe 340-acre data center in Warrenton with conditions

summary: Gateway Digital Campus and Provident Data Centers are seeking to rezone roughly 600 acres of agricultural land near I-44 and Robertsville Road in Villa Ridge, Missouri, for a hyperscale data center, sitting directly adjacent to the Shaw Nature Reserve. The project returned to the Franklin County Planning & Zoning Commission on March 17, 2026 despite being tabled for 6 months in January. Both Gateway and the Beltline Energy project are on the same March 17 agenda, alongside proposed county-wide data center regulations including 400ft setbacks, 800-acre site limits, and sound limits.
objective: Block Gateway Digital Campus rezoning of 600 acres of farmland

opposition_groups: Osawatomie Residents Concerned About Data Centers
summary: The city of Osawatomie, Kansas, entered a pre-development agreement with Alcove Development/Northland Data Centers for a $900 million to $1.1 billion data center project on 115 acres of city-owned land, requiring one million gallons of water and 150MW of power per day. Residents have pushed back, citing the city's aging water infrastructure, fears of noise, and lack of community input. The Miami County Commission is considering a temporary moratorium countywide while proper regulations are developed.
objective: Oppose Alcove Development data center over water in Osawatomie

summary: Nebraska legislators introduced a bill that would allow privately built power generation facilities (1,000+ MW) to serve large industrial customers like data centers, with eminent domain protections. The bill was drafted to enable Google's proposed fourth Nebraska data center requiring 1,000-3,000 MW of power, powered by a privately built natural gas plant. The bill is pending in the Legislature.
objective: Prohibit eminent domain for data center land acquisition

opposition_groups: Say NO to Data Centers in Troy IL
summary: The Troy, Illinois, Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers in March 2026, following the collapse of Cloverleaf Infrastructure's proposal for a 1.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center sited less than a mile from neighborhoods, daycares, and senior living facilities. Hundreds of residents packed a meeting at Triad High School in February to challenge the project. The moratorium prevents new data center applications while the planning commission strengthens zoning ordinances. Troy Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers on March 13, after Cloverleaf Infrastructure's 500MW proposal collapsed following massive public opposition. Hundreds of residents packed Triad High School in February. City Council final passage expected March 16.
objective: Halt data centers in Troy after Cloverleaf Infrastructure proposal

summary: HydraVault received a full building permit from Chicago, Illinois, to construct a 76,000-square-foot, 20-megawatt AI data center at 2538 S. Wabash Avenue in the South Loop. Residents reported opposition due to energy consumption, water use, and the lack of public hearings — the project was classified as a 'minor change' by zoning officials, bypassing community review.
objective: Oppose HydraVault data center over water in Chicago South Loop

opposition_groups: Block Project Steel and Project Cardinal
summary: After a nearly six-hour contentious city council meeting, Yorkville, Illinois, approved Pioneer Development's 'Project Cardinal' — a 1,037-acre, 1.8-gigawatt data center campus with 14 buildings totaling 17 million square feet, plus a $51 million upfront payment. Residents spoke against it, citing water usage, noise, air quality, and property value concerns. Yorkville City Council approved Project Cardinal at 12:51 AM on March 12 after a nearly 6-hour contentious meeting. The 1,037-acre campus (Pioneer Development LLC) includes 14 buildings totaling 17M sq ft. Development agreement includes $51M in upfront payments over 4 years and $68.25M directed to the school district ($91M total). First data center operational by 2029.
objective: Rally against Pioneer Development data center in Yorkville

summary: Cloverleaf Infrastructure has been in detailed discussions with Edwardsville, Illinois, officials since mid-2025 about a data center south of New Poag Road. Obtained emails revealed extensive behind-the-scenes planning despite public assurances of 'no formal proposal.' Residents for Responsible Growth organized a meeting of nearly 300 people in March 2026.
objective: Rally against Cloverleaf Infrastructure data center in Edwardsville

summary: Sprigg Township, Ohio, trustees passed a 12-month voluntary moratorium on data centers in late February 2026, in response to a proposed facility near the former Stuart power plant requiring 1,300 megawatts — 31 times Adams County's annual power consumption. First of two Adams County localities to act, followed by Monroe Township.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Sprigg Township

summary: Ashville, Ohio, Village Council unanimously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on December 20, 2025, in response to EdgeConneX's proposal on 680 acres including an on-site 200-megawatt natural gas power plant. Residents lined yards with 'No Data Center' signs. EdgeConneX withdrew its annexation application but reportedly already holds land zoned for data center use.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Ashville (EdgeConneX)

summary: Columbus City Council held hearings in March 2026 examining rapid data center growth in central Ohio, with large facilities potentially requiring up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Of 130 data centers in the area, only 18 fall within city limits, limiting regulatory authority. Some councilmembers called for a moratorium though no legislation has been introduced.
objective: Hold council hearings examining data center water use and growth in Columbus

summary: Senate Bill 969, authored by Sen. Andre Jacque (R-New Franken), would prohibit NDAs between data center companies and municipalities, while still allowing withholding of genuine trade secrets. The bill was prompted by a Wisconsin Watch investigation revealing at least four communities had signed secrecy deals hiding billion-dollar data center plans. Rep. Clint Moses (R-Menomonie), author of the companion Assembly bill, called the measure 'really about trust.' Passed Senate Utilities Committee 4-1, but the Senate adjourned the 2026 session without a floor vote.
objective: Ban data center NDAs in Wisconsin (original bipartisan bill)

summary: Tulsa, Oklahoma, City Councilor Laura Bellis proposed a 365-day moratorium on new data center construction citywide (by Meta / Beale Infrastructure). The ordinance would pause acceptance, processing, and issuance of building permits for data centers while a working group studies impacts. The moratorium was heard at the March 11 council meeting and a final vote is scheduled for March 25, 2026. Bellis says not all colleagues are on board. The moratorium would not affect existing Project Anthem ($800M, 340 acres in east Tulsa). Bellis is open to amendments including a shorter duration. Tulsa City Council passed the moratorium unanimously on March 25, amended to 9 months (through Dec 31, 2026). Exempts Project Anthem phases 1-2 and Project Clydesdale.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Tulsa

summary: SB 1488, sponsored by Republican Sen. Sacchieri, would have imposed a three-year moratorium on data centers with electrical load greater than 100 MW until November 1, 2029. Directed the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to study impacts on water supply, utility rates, property values, and ideal siting locations. The bill missed key legislative deadlines and died without advancing from committee.
objective: Pause data center permits for study via SB 1488

summary: Mat-Su Borough, Alaska, Assembly passed a resolution 5-2 on March 2, 2026 to partner with Terra Energy Center Corp. to market borough-owned land near Big Lake and Port MacKenzie for data center or industrial development. Mayor Edna DeVries vetoed the resolution, citing concerns about entering a marketing agreement without full vetting, unapproved development locations, and lack of conditional use permit discussion. Override requires at least five Assembly votes at the next regular meeting.
objective: Veto borough resolution marketing public land for Terra Energy Center data center development

summary: Shreveport, Louisiana, Metropolitan Planning Commission denied a special use permit for a 2.8 million sq ft Franks Investment Company LLC data center at 7340 Greenwood Road (Resilient Technology Park) on a 4-4 vote on December 3, 2025, after major community backlash over noise, water use (7.5M gallons/day), and environmental impacts. The Shreveport City Council overturned the denial on December 18, 2025 in a unanimous 7-0 vote, and separately approved selling up to 7.5M gallons of water daily to the facility. A subsequent lawsuit was filed accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations.
objective: Deny Franks Investment Company conditional use permit in Shreveport

summary: Hermiston, Oregon, City Council voted in September 2025 to annex 800 acres of land to attract more data centers, with only one councilor (Jackie Linton) voting against. Linton raised concerns about water supply impacts and inadequate review time. Amazon Web Services already has dozens of data centers across Umatilla and Morrow counties and is building two more in Hermiston. The annexation was approved over community objections.
objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center over water in Hermiston

summary: Oklahoma HB 2992, the Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act of 2026 (Rep. Brad Boles R-Marlow / Sen. Green R-Wellston), passed the House floor 92-2 in March 2026 and now moves to the Senate. The bill defines 'large load customers' as new facilities adding ≥75 MW, requires electric suppliers to establish separate terms for them including credit protections and a minimum 10-year service commitment, and clarifies residential/commercial/traditional industrial customers are not included. 23 House and Senate lawmakers from both parties have signed on as co-authors. Oklahoma currently has 5 operating data centers with 18 MW; 10 planned projects would add 1,700 MW.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via HB 2992

summary: Michigan Senate Bills 761, 762, and 763 (Sens. Rosemary Bayer D-West Bloomfield, Sue Shink D-Northfield Township, Erika Geiss D-Taylor) introduced December 2025. SB 761 bans data centers withdrawing >2 million gallons/day for consumptive use (water that evaporates or doesn't return to systems). SB 762 requires the Michigan PSC to publish annual reports on total energy spending and water usage of data centers. SB 763 prohibits passing infrastructure improvement costs on to residential ratepayers. As of March 2026, none of the bills have passed out of committee. Separately, Reps. Wortz, Fox, and Wegela introduced a bipartisan three-bill House package (the 'Data Center Regulation Act') that would prohibit new data centers from receiving permits or commencing operations until April 1, 2027. Speaker Matt Hall and Gov. Whitmer have not commented, raising doubts about passage.
objective: Ban data center water withdrawals over 2M gallons/day (SB 761-763)

summary: The Montana Right to Compute Act (SB 212), signed by Governor Gianforte on April 16, 2025, establishes a legal right to own and use computational resources including data centers. The law requires any government regulation restricting computing resources to be 'demonstrably necessary and narrowly tailored,' effectively limiting local governments' ability to restrict data center siting or operations through zoning. The law was enacted without significant organized opposition, representing a setback for community control advocates.
objective: Establish right to compute, limiting local data center regulation

summary: Mississippi SB 3168, enacted July 1, 2025, expands the state's data center tax incentive program. The law grants sales and use tax exemptions to data centers investing $250M+ and creating 35+ jobs at 125% of average state wages, adds electricity purchases to exempt 'state taxes,' provides automatic 10-year extension eligibility for qualifying expansions ($100M+), and excludes cryptocurrency mining. The bill passed both chambers and was signed into law without significant opposition.
objective: Create data center tax incentive program in MS

summary: Clean Cloud Act of 2025 (S. 1475), introduced by Senate Democrats, establishes an emissions standard and fee system for large data centers and cryptomining facilities. Beginning January 1, 2026, the EPA Administrator assesses fees on covered facilities. Region-specific emissions baselines reduce annually by 11% from 2026 through 2034, reaching net-zero by 2035. Facilities exceeding baselines pay a fee starting at $20/metric ton CO2e, rising annually by inflation plus $10. Annual data collection required for facilities with >100 kW IT power. Collected fees: 3% admin, 25% grants offsetting residential energy costs, 70% grants/loans for zero-carbon energy and long-duration storage. As of early 2026, the bill remains in committee with little movement.
objective: Set federal emissions standards for data center electricity

summary: Executive Order 14318, 'Accelerating Federal Permitting of Data Center Infrastructure,' was signed by President Trump on July 23, 2025. The order directs agencies to streamline NEPA reviews, establish new categorical exclusions for data center and energy projects, and designate qualifying projects ($500M+ capital or 100+ MW load) as 'transparency projects' for expedited FAST-41 review. It authorizes use of federal land for data centers, with DOE selecting four initial sites, and directs the Commerce Department to provide financial support including loans, grants, and tax incentives. The order was enacted without Congressional action.
objective: Fast-track federal permitting for $500M+ data center and energy projects via EO 14318

summary: Virginia HB 784 provides additional transparency by requiring an annual report disclosing information about each data center operator that receives a sales tax exemption, including whether they've met job and investment goals. Part of Virginia's massive 2026 legislative push: lawmakers considered 61 data center bills, sent 15 to the governor's desk, and carried over 46 to 2027. As of late March 2026, the data center tax exemption (~$1.6B/year) remains unresolved as the Senate seeks to eliminate it entirely while the House wants to tie it to environmental compliance. This budget standoff has delayed the state budget; a special session is set for April 23 to resolve the issue. Among bills passed: HB 507 (Tier IV generators required for new data centers, awaiting governor) and HB 1393 (rate-recovery for new generation costs from large electric demand customers).
bill_name: HB 784 - Data Center Tax Exemption Reporting
objective: Mandate transparency and reporting from data centers in VA

summary: Virginia SB 393, the Virginia's Great Outdoors Act (companion HB 641), was defeated in February 2026. The bill would have created a $250M/year dedicated funding stream for land conservation, public lands, trails, and outdoor recreation funded by a $3/sq ft annual tax on data centers above 25,000 sq ft. The Senate Finance Resources Subcommittee voted to 'carry over' SB 393, delaying it indefinitely. Both HB 641 and SB 393 have been defeated. The proposal would have established Virginia's largest-ever conservation fund, including a Virginia Tribal Commitment Fund, and was supported by PEC, NVCT, and conservation groups statewide.
objective: Impose $3/sq ft tax on data centers above 25,000 sq ft for conservation funding

summary: Prohibits Virginia utilities from recovering costs of transmission lines and energy generation serving data centers over 100 MW from residential ratepayers. Aimed at addressing the $4.4 billion in data center transmission costs borne by customers across seven PJM states in 2024 alone. House subcommittee recommended continuing to next session. Part of a broader package of 2026 Virginia data center bills targeting ratepayer protections and industry accountability.
bill_name: HB 503 - Data Center Cost Recovery Prohibition
objective: Prohibit utility cost recovery for 100MW+ data center transmission from residential ratepayers

summary: Illinois SB 2182, sponsored by Sen. Elgie Sims Jr. (D), would require data centers receiving state tax credits to enter into labor peace agreements with unions representing workers who operate and maintain critical systems. Under LPAs, employers agree not to interfere with unionization efforts while unions agree not to strike. The bill would add labor requirements to Illinois's existing Data Center Investment Program and is advancing through the Senate.
objective: Require labor peace agreements for data center tax credits

summary: In Virginia, Henrico County's Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested rights claim for a data center complex on nearly 200 acres along Darbytown Road in the Varina district on Feb. 26, 2026. CEO David Wagner had filed the claim in October 2025, Arguing the company had a right to build under the pre-June 2025 rules. But a key factor was that Wagner did not purchase the site until November 2025 -- a month after filing his claim. The company had previously withdrawn its provisional use permit application. Henrico adopted new rules in June 2025 requiring all hyperscale data centers to go through public hearings. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.
objective: Uphold denial of Centra Logistics data center on appeal

summary: Reston-based developer WestDulles Properties filed an appeal in March 2026 of Chesterfield County staff's determination that a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) is required before its 170-acre data center development at 1500 Battery Brooke Parkway in the James River Industrial Center can be built. Chesterfield Planning Director Andy Gillies determined the project requires a CUP as of January 1, 2026 under the new zoning ordinance making data centers conditional uses in industrially zoned land. WestDulles argues no CUP is needed because the site is zoned I-3 Heavy Industrial, where data centers were a by-right use under the previous ordinance. The matter is slated to go before the Chesterfield Board of Zoning Appeals in May 2026. Mass-grading plan was received by the county in fall 2025.
objective: Require use permit for WestDulles Properties data center in Chesterfield County

summary: Powhatan County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted 5-0 on Oct. 29, 2025 to approve rezoning of approximately 61 additional acres to expand the planned Province Group data center campus from 120 to 181 acres (1.5M to 2M sq ft). This came despite the Planning Commission's unanimous recommendation of denial on Oct. 7. The $2.7B campus near the Powhatan-Chesterfield line could eventually house up to four data center buildings requiring 300-400 MW of power and a new substation. Estimated annual tax revenue at full build-out: $9.7 million. The original 120-acre campus was approved 3-2 in October 2024.
objective: Block Province Group rezoning of 181 acres in Powhatan County

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: Amazon proposed three 110-foot-tall data center buildings totaling nearly 1.1 million sq ft on 52 acres along Ashton Avenue near Manassas Mall, sandwiched between three schools and a townhome development. The Prince William County, Virginia, Planning Commission voted 6-1 in June 2024 to recommend denial due to noise, wildlife, and neighborhood proximity concerns. The Board of County Supervisors shelved the proposal indefinitely in October 2024 and it was deferred again in January 2025 to a date uncertain. Amazon maintains it still plans to build on the site but no hearing date is set.
objective: Deny Amazon Web Services data center on 52 acres in Prince William

summary: DeKalb County, Tennessee, Commission is considering data center regulations proposed by County Mayor Matt Adcock after receiving an inquiry about locating a nuclear-powered data center in the county. The proposed resolution would prohibit data centers within 5,000 feet of residences, schools, daycares, parks, churches, commercial, or industrial establishments. Applicants would need permits with appeal rights. Commissioner Tom Chandler called the resolution 'a sham' arguing the 5,000-foot restriction makes permit approval impossible anywhere in the county. The action was tabled to allow public input; no final vote recorded.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in DeKalb County

opposition_groups: West Virginia Center on Budget & Policy
summary: Would restore local government control over data center siting in West Virginia, reversing HB 2014 — the Power Generation and Consumption Act signed by Gov. Morrisey — which allows data centers to operate on independent microgrids and strips counties of jurisdiction over siting and size. Tucker County residents pushed for the bill amid growing backlash over development in their communities. Never received a committee hearing during the session that ended March 14, 2026.
bill_name: SB 658 - Data Center Local Control Restoration
objective: Restore local government authority over data centers in WV

summary: The Virginia Senate budget proposes accelerating the data center tax exemption sunset from 2035 to January 2027, which would redirect $1.6B/year to tax rebates, transportation, and water infrastructure. The House budget preserves the exemption but adds clean energy requirements. The Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition, Piedmont Environmental Council, and Good Jobs First are pushing for the accelerated sunset. The General Assembly reconvenes April 22 to resolve the stalemate.
bill_name: FY2027-28 Budget - Data Center Tax Exemption
objective: Accelerate data center tax exemption sunset in VA

summary: The Maricopa County, Arizona, Board of Supervisors unanimously approved a modernized zoning ordinance establishing specific rules for data center development, effective January 9, 2026. The new rules limit data centers to two industrial zoning districts (IND-2 and IND-3), requiring full rezoning for any other land.
objective: Block data center rezoning in Maricopa County

summary: In Colorado, CoreSite is building a 600,000-square-foot data center in Elyria-Swansea, One of the most polluted zip codes in the country (80216), which proceeded as a by-right use without community review. Hundreds packed a February 2026 meeting demanding transparency, and delivered a 'Good Neighbor' proposal. Denver's mayor subsequently joined calls for a moratorium.
objective: Mobilize opposition to CoreSite data center in Denver

summary: Tucson, Arizona, city officials began developing new zoning regulations in February 2026 for data centers over 50,000 square feet. The proposed rules would require water and power disclosure from developers and empower the city to fine or shut off water if usage exceeds allotment by 120%. Heavy industrial zones were designated as the most suitable locations. The Tucson Clean Energy Coalition supported the initiative. The regulations are pending final adoption.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Tucson

summary: Despite community opposition from RAZE and the Ak-Chin Indian Community, Pinal County, Arizona, approved three major data center projects in November 2025 totaling ~3,900 acres, including the $33 billion Vermaland 'La Osa' project near Eloy. Residents raised concerns about desert water use, noise, cultural impacts to tribal land, and infrastructure strain.
objective: Challenge three data center approvals totaling 3,900 acres in Pinal County

opposition_groups: Iron County Citizens for Responsible Growth
summary: In Iron County, Utah, 350+ residents packed Festival Hall on March 5, 2026 for a hearing on the Antelope Data Center proposed for 640 acres on Antelope Springs Road, 15 miles NW of Cedar City. Of 15+ speakers over 2 hours, only one supported the project. Concerns: water (2.6M gallon initial fill), utility prices, farmland loss, noise, pollution.
objective: Block Antelope Data Center on 640 acres near Cedar City over water and farmland loss

opposition_groups: Millard County Residents Against Solar Farm
summary: Millard County, Utah, Commission approved rezoning of 4,400+ acres from agricultural to forest/range for Creekstone Energy solar farm to power AI data centers, despite resident opposition. Concerns about ruining land's multipurpose use, visual impact, and long-term consequences. The project involves 4400 acres. Opposition was led by Millard County Residents Against Solar Farm. Key concerns driving this action include environmental impacts and noise impacts.
objective: Rezone 4,400+ acres from agricultural for Creekstone Energy solar farm powering AI data centers

opposition_groups: Empowerment Congress of Doña Ana County
summary: Two lawsuits filed against Doña Ana County, New Mexico, over 'Project Jupiter' — a 1,400-acre data center complex near Sunland Park with $165B in Industrial Revenue Bonds (by OpenAI). Empowerment Congress and NMELC allege Open Meetings Act violations and improper LEDA tax rebates. Project includes natural gas power plants and desalination plant. Could emit more greenhouse gases than Albuquerque and Las Cruces combined.
objective: Sue over open meeting violations in OpenAI approval

summary: In Arizona, HB 2795 would prohibit counties (<500K pop) from denying zoning approval for small modular reactors after federal design certification and site permitting. Allows SMR construction colocated with large industrial users (data centers) without ACC environmental compatibility certificate. Cleared House on party-line vote Feb 26, advancing to Senate. Gov. Hobbs vetoed similar bill in 2025.
objective: Allow SMR colocation with data centers in Arizona counties

summary: CyrusOne filed plans for a 400-megawatt data center campus in Whitney, Hill County, with an initial $750 million building due by October 2026. Residents packed a town hall citing water use from Lake Whitney (a petition with 6,000 signatures), noise, and tax abatements. The opposition has become a key issue in the 2026 Republican primary for Texas Senate District 22.
objective: 6000-signature petition opposing data center in Hill County

opposition_groups: Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers
summary: Amazon is building a 1-million-square-foot data center on 1,800 acres at the Keystone Trade Center in Falls Township, Bucks County, fast-tracked through Governor Shapiro's PA Permit Fast Track Program. Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing energy grid strain, increased electricity rates, and diesel generator impacts. A regional survey found 42% of SE Pennsylvania residents object to data centers vs 34% in support. The project was approved despite community concerns.
objective: Oppose Amazon data center in Falls Township

summary: Bryan, Texas, City Council proactively annexed 162 acres near the RELLIS campus and adopted a Planned Development zoning district allowing data centers with stricter-than-normal standards including increased setbacks, noise requirements, and screening specifications. No data center project has been formally proposed yet. The preemptive zoning measures represent a community win in establishing regulations before development pressure arrives.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Bryan

summary: The Oneonta, New York, Town Board passed a 12-month moratorium on AI data centers in March 2026 after residents presented more than 700 signatures, prompted by Eco-Yotta Inc.'s plans to rezone 150 acres of farmland. This came weeks after the nearby Town of Dryden instituted an outright ban following its own 18-month moratorium.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Oneonta (Eco-Yotta)

summary: Verdantas/Shelbourne proposed an 847,000 sq ft data center complex on a 44-acre site at the White Clay Center Industrial Park off Ogletown Road in Newark, DE. The proposal would demolish seven existing buildings and replace them with a three-building data center campus. Site is owned by NY-based Shelbourne (8M sq ft commercial real estate portfolio). The location is outside Delaware's Coastal Zone and is in zoning that already allows data center projects. Critically, the New Castle County Council passed new data center regulations in March 2026 that explicitly do NOT apply to the White Clay project (or other in-pipeline proposals) — only to newly proposed projects. The project remains in early stages of approval.
objective: Block 847,000 sq ft data center replacing White Clay Corporate Center in Newark

summary: Grant County Commissioners passed a 24-month moratorium on data centers on March 2, 2026. The Area Plan Commission began drafting ordinance language focusing on transparency, resource impacts, and local benefits, including questions about client disclosure and annual energy/water reporting. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition of Indiana. The community successfully achieved its objective.
objective: Impose 24-month data centers moratorium in Grant County

summary: 795-acre data center (Hillwood/PowerHouse) on south Joliet farmland above depleting aquifer. Plan Commission approved 7-1 despite 4+ hours of opposition testimony. Hundreds packed City Hall. Would be largest data center in Illinois. Plan Commission approved 7-1 on March 5. At the March 17 City Council meeting, a marathon 6.5-hour public hearing forced a recess -- vote delayed to Thursday March 19 due to election day restrictions. Developers promise $100M community contribution, 7,000-10,000 construction jobs, and 700 permanent jobs. Would be the largest data center in Illinois at $20B, 1.8GW, 795 acres. Joliet City Council approved the $20B PowerHouse/Hillwood campus 8-1 on March 20 after a 6.5-hour public hearing. Only Councilmember Suzanna Ibarra voted no.
objective: Rally against Hillwood data center in Joliet

summary: Lake County, Indiana, Council approved stringent data center regulations: only permitted in industrial zones with special approval, 200-ft setbacks from residential/agricultural, 55dB noise max, underground utilities, decommissioning plans, and annual firefighter training requirements. The project involves 160 acres. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition and Hoosier Action. Key concerns driving this action include zoning concerns. The community successfully achieved its objective.
objective: Impose 55 dB noise limit on data centers in Lake County

bill_name: Senate Democrats Data Center Accountability Package
summary: Sen. Kent Smith (D-Euclid) introduced a package of 6+ bills targeting data center impacts in Ohio: the Data Center Grid Cost Responsibility Act, a Tax Break Ban, the Powering Ohio Communities Act, the Local Development Protection Act, the Power Reliability and Fairness Act, and the Responsible Water Use Act which would cap daily water use at 5 million gallons. The Ohio Consumers' Counsel endorsed the package. The bills are pending committee action.
objective: Six-bill legislative package addressing data center grid costs, tax breaks, and water use in Ohio

summary: Thomas County, Georgia, Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on AI data center construction in January 2026. Commission Chair Mark NeSmith said the board was 'certainly not anti-data centers' but preferred to be proactive in protecting the community while gathering more information.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Thomas County

summary: Google proposed a 1GW data center on 546 acres near Irwinville in an agricultural zone. The Irwin County, Georgia, Planning Commission voted to recommend approval on Feb 26, 2026. However, the applicant withdrew the application on March 2, 2026 before the County Commission could vote, without prejudice to filing a similar application in the future. Opposition was led by Suwannee Riverkeeper/WWALS Watershed Coalition and concerned residents who raised alarms about water usage (1M+ gallons/day), impacts on the Alapaha River, noise, and farmland loss. Following the withdrawal, Irwin County began developing a data center ordinance, with a public hearing held March 17.
objective: Petition to block Google data center in Irwin County

summary: Clarksdale, Mississippi, Board of Commissioners voted on March 24, 2026, to rezone a 648-acre site between Sunflower River and Roundyard neighborhood from commercial/agricultural to light industrial for a potential data center. The rezoning came with three conditions: a one-time $5 million impact fee, the developer paying for all public utility improvements, and a 1,000-foot setback from residential areas. Earlier board meetings in January were contentious, with the board tabling the vote after a failed motion. No developer has yet filed an application. The site lies along U.S. 278 and Tallahatchie Street, and the vote is described as only 'the beginning of the conversation' about data centers in Clarksdale.
objective: Block rezoning of 648-acre data center site near Sunflower River in Clarksdale

summary: Alabama HB 399, sponsored by Rep. Hulsey (R-Helena), is the House companion to SB 265. Limits tax abatements for data center projects over $200M to 20 years, down from the current 30-year maximum. Requires data centers consuming 100+ MW to pay state sales and use taxes allocated to the General Fund, effective January 1, 2027. Passed House 103-0. Part of a bipartisan effort to rein in data center subsidies after the Meridian Compass deal was named '2025 Worst Economic Development Deal.'
objective: Limit data center tax abatements to 20 years and require 100MW+ sales tax payments (HB 399)

opposition_groups: Nobles County Residents Against Data Centers
summary: Geronimo Power proposed a data center near Reading in Nobles County, Minnesota. The Planning Commission voted against allowing data centers in the agricultural preservation district. A standing-room-only public hearing on February 4, 2026 drew widespread opposition. Commissioners approved an AUAR environmental review 4-1 but discussed a moratorium on data centers. The Nobles County Residents Against Data Centers organized the opposition. The outcome is pending.
objective: Reject data centers in agricultural preservation district and pursue environmental review in Nobles County

summary: The Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy (MCEA) filed an appeal against the City of Faribault over an 'inadequate' environmental review of Archer Datacenters' proposal on 87.6 acres. MCEA alleges the developer dropped estimated greenhouse gas emissions by 98% between draft and final stages without explanation, and failed to analyze cumulative impacts from the wave of nearby data center proposals. The appeal is active and pending resolution.
objective: Challenge data center air permits in Faribault

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: Prince William County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted to eliminate by-right approval for data centers in the Data Center Opportunity Zone Overlay District, requiring special use permits for all future facilities. Republican Supervisor Tom Gordy said the overlay 'has outlived its useful purpose' and suggested ending it outright. The same meeting controversially approved the Hornbaker Road data center outside the overlay in a 4-3 vote despite heated public opposition.
objective: End by-right data center approvals in Prince William overlay zone

summary: Culpeper County, Virginia, unanimously required conditional use permits for data centers on industrial land in September 2025, impacting 3,000+ acres across 140+ parcels that previously allowed by-right construction. Five projects in the Culpeper Technology Zone remain vested. The Piedmont Environmental Council and Coalition to Save Culpeper pushed for the stronger regulations.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Culpeper County

summary: GigaLand LLC resubmitted a scaled-down data center proposal near Remington after withdrawing the original in September 2025, reducing building space by 55% (from 2.2M to 998,662 sq ft) and cutting substation capacity from 1.2 GW to 600 MW, with a $3 billion estimated cost on 202 acres. Opposition groups including Protect Fauquier, Virginia, and the Piedmont Environmental Council continue monitoring.
objective: Block GigaLand LLC's $3B, 600 MW data center on 202 acres near Remington

summary: Authorizes a federal study on the environmental, economic, and public health impacts of data centers, with a focus on communities of color and low-income communities. Requires the Secretary of Energy to conduct the study and report findings to Congress. Endorsed by NRDC, Climate Justice Alliance, GreenLatinos, Food & Water Watch.
objective: Authorize federal study on data center impacts in communities of color and low-income areas

summary: In Iowa, originally filed as HF 2447, Renumbered to HF 2690 after committee approval on Feb 23, 2026. Amendment H-8214 filed March 12. Key provisions: quarterly water/energy reporting, public disclosure within 30 days, separate utility rate class for data centers using 20+ MW. Sponsored by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids). First-of-its-kind legislation targeting water utilities specifically.
objective: Require quarterly water and energy reporting from data centers using 20+ MW in Iowa (HF 2690)

summary: The PRICE Act (Preventing Rate Inflation in Consumer Energy), HR6983, introduced by Reps. Rob Menendez (D-NJ) and Greg Casar (D-TX), would require data center operators to generate all electricity on-site rather than drawing from the grid. The bill mandates 75% renewable energy by 2035 and 100% by 2040, with civil penalties up to $100,000 per day for violations. Endorsed by the Consumer Federation of America and Evergreen Action, the companion Data Center Transparency Act (HR6984) would require EPA and EIA to collect quarterly environmental and energy data from every US data center.
objective: Require data centers to generate 100% of electricity on-site

summary: HR 6179, the Clean Cloud Act of 2025, was introduced by Rep. Steve Cohen (D-TN) — the House companion to S.1475 by Senate Democrats. Establishes emission standards for high-demand energy users (data centers and cryptominers). Beginning 2026, mandates collection of detailed electricity usage and emissions data from facilities >100 kW installed power. Beginning 2026, fees on electricity utilities serving these facilities based on GHG emissions, base rate $20/ton above regional baseline, increasing annually by inflation plus $10. Region-specific baselines reduce 11%/year through 2034 reaching net-zero by 2035. Referred to House Energy and Commerce Committee. Bill remains in committee with little movement as of early 2026. Companion to Sen. Whitehouse's S.1475.
objective: Establish emissions fee system for data centers (House companion)

bill_name: H.R. 5227 - Unleashing Low-Cost Rural AI Act
summary: HR 5227, the Unleashing Low-Cost Rural AI Act (Rep. Moore), would direct the Secretary of Energy to designate a National Laboratory to study the impact of AI and data center site growth on US energy supply resources. Study would identify rural areas poised for tech expansions and assess impact on energy supply, reliability, and consumer costs. Examines whether infrastructure updates are needed to support co-location of AI/data centers, and feasibility of using alternative energy sources (hydroelectric, solar, wind, battery storage, carbon capture, nuclear, geothermal). Bill is in 119th Congress (2025-2026). Awaiting committee action.
objective: Commission legislative study on data center impacts in US

summary: DOE Section 403 directive: On October 23, 2025, US DOE Secretary Chris Wright issued a formal directive to FERC under Section 403 of the DOE Organization Act to initiate rulemaking for an Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (ANOPR) titled 'Ensuring the Timely and Orderly Interconnection of Large Loads' — proposing FERC assert jurisdiction over interconnection of large loads >20 MW (an area traditionally regulated by states). Directive sets a final rule deadline of April 30, 2026. FERC opened a notice-and-comment period (initial comments due Nov 14, 2025; reply comments Nov 28, 2025). Section 403 authority lets the Secretary 'propose' rules but does not require FERC to implement. Critics warn this could pre-empt state oversight of grid impacts from data centers.
objective: DOE order standardizing large-load grid interconnection rules

summary: FERC issued an order in December 2025 directing PJM, the nation's largest grid operator, to establish transparent rules to facilitate service of AI-driven data centers and other large loads co-located with generating facilities. The Commission found PJM's tariff was unjust and unreasonable due to insufficient clarity on rates/terms for generators serving co-located load. FERC directed PJM to create three new transmission services: interim non-firm; firm contract demand; and non-firm contract demand. PJM compliance filing deadlines: January 19-20, 2026 (initial filings on provisional interconnection and reliability concerns), February 16, 2026 (additional tariff revisions), with paper hearing initial briefs Feb 16, responses Mar 18, replies Apr 17. Setting precedent for behind-the-meter generation and 'colocation' of data centers with power plants.
objective: Order PJM to develop data center colocation rules at power plants

summary: New York S6394A, the Sustainable Data Centers Act, would regulate energy consumption by data centers in the state. The bill requires annual disclosure reporting, prohibits fossil fuel power purchase agreements, and directs the Public Service Commission to establish a surcharge/discount plan. It mandates a progressive transition to renewable energy: 33% by 2030, 67% by 2035, and 100% by 2040. The bill is pending committee action.
objective: Mandate renewable energy transition and ban fossil fuel PPAs for data centers in New York

summary: New York S8540, the Accountability for Computing and Data Centers (ACDC) Act, would require electric and gas corporations to establish an independent classification of service for large energy use facilities separate from other rate classes. The bill authorizes DPS to develop financial surety requirements and prevents data center costs from being blended into residential rate cases. The bill is pending in the Senate.
objective: Create separate utility rate class for data centers in New York

summary: In New Hampshire, HB 1724, Sponsored by Rep. Wendy Thomas, Requires public utilities to submit annual reports detailing transmission costs, wholesale electricity market impacts, and generation capacity adequacy, with specific analysis of data center-related demand. Mandates public hearings and review by the state energy agency. Referred to the House Science, Technology and Energy Committee with a public hearing held February 10, 2026.
objective: Require utility reporting of data center transmission impacts in NH

summary: Wyoming HB0090 would have appropriated $500K to the State Engineer to study how large-scale industrial water use by data centers, carbon capture, and hydrogen projects impacts the state's water supply. The bill advanced 8-1 out of the House Agriculture Committee and received House Appropriations Committee approval on February 16, 2026, but did not pass the full chamber. The study was part of growing concern in Wyoming as data center developers eye the state for its cheap land and energy resources.
objective: Appropriate $500K to study large-scale industrial water use by data centers in Wyoming

summary: In Mississippi, HB 1635 was introduced to redirect 80% of local ad-valorem taxes collected above $1B from large projects (including data centers) into a new state infrastructure and economic development fund, with 20% to local government and schools. Madison County Economic Development Authority director Joey Deason objected to the measure, warning it would slash his county's projected $70M/year in new Amazon data center tax revenue to just $20M/year. The author did not bring the bill to committee vote.
objective: Redirect data center tax revenue to state fund

summary: Menlo Equities proposed a data center on 40.6 acres in the Ahwatukee, Arizona, Foothills area of Phoenix. The Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing concerns about water use in the desert, noise, and environmental impacts on the residential community. The project remains ongoing with the outcome pending.
objective: Oppose Menlo Equities data center over water in Ahwatukee
opposition_groups: Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers

summary: Tract is developing the 2,019-acre Buckeye Technology Park, the largest contiguous data center project in the Phoenix market, located near Palo Verde nuclear power plant and the I-10 corridor in Buckeye, AZ. Could see up to 20 million sq ft of data center space across as many as 40 individual data centers at full buildout. Tract is currently working with the local utility on plans to support up to 1.8 GW of capacity. $20 billion total investment over a 15-year buildout, currently in planning stage. Tract acquired the 2,069-acre site from Arizona Land Consulting for $136M+ (which had bought it for $40M in 2022). Buckeye is considering a development agreement reimbursing Tract up to $50M in public infrastructure improvements (water, sewer, streets, transportation), funded through 20% of tax revenues over 20 years.
objective: 100-signature petition opposing Tract data center
opposition_groups: Buckeye Residents Against Data Centers (ad hoc)

summary: Oppidan proposed a 5MW data center on a 7-acre site in the Stead area of Reno, Nevada,. The Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter raised environmental, grid, and water concerns. Despite opposition, the project was approved, with community advocates failing to block the permit. The community's stated objective was to deny Oppidan 5MW data center permit in Reno.
objective: Deny Oppidan 5MW data center permit in Reno

summary: DCPC's Summit Ridge Data Center, a 100 MW off-grid natural gas-powered facility in Santaquin, Utah, was approved by City Council in Oct 2024 without adequate public awareness. Residents rallied outside City Hall, feeling 'blindsided.' A Change.org petition has 1,350+ signatures. Concerns about air quality from natural gas generators, noise, and lack of transparency.
objective: Petition to block DCPC data center in Santaquin

summary: Lowell City Council voted 10-0 in January 2026 for a one-year moratorium on data center construction or expansion, becoming the first Massachusetts city to enact such a measure. The Markley Group converted a former Prince Pasta factory in the Sacred Heart neighborhood — a state-designated environmental justice zone — into a data center in 2015 with $77M in tax incentives. Since 2022, Markley has applied for permits to add 20 diesel generators (bringing the total to 27) and 16 cooling towers, prompting years of resident complaints about noise and diesel fumes. The formal 360-day moratorium was adopted 10-0 on March 11, 2026, with an option to extend by 180 days.
objective: Impose 360-day moratorium on Markley Group data center expansion

summary: Gibraltar, Michigan, City Council unanimously approved a year-long moratorium in March 2026, freezing a proposed 100 MW data center at the former McLouth Steel site (27800 W. Jefferson Ave.) by Raeden. The facility would consume as much energy as 80,000 homes. Residents and Downriver activists raised concerns about increased electric bills and impacts on nearby Humbug Marsh wildlife refuge. The developer proposed addressing contamination at an adjacent Superfund property and partnering with DTE Energy for on-site power generation.
objective: Halt data centers in Gibraltar after Raeden proposal

summary: Scioto Township trustees in Pickaway County enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development after a heated public meeting (by EdgeConneX). Trustees debated duration, ultimately agreeing a full year was necessary to craft legally sound zoning regulations. Concerns centered on rising residential electricity costs, environmental strain from water usage, and noise pollution. Part of a broader wave across Ohio where generous sales tax exemptions made rural communities prime targets.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Scioto Township

summary: The Village of DeForest, Wisconsin, announced on January 27 that QTS Data Centers' proposed $12 billion data center was 'not feasible.' The project required annexing 1,600 acres from the Town of Vienna, but a state Department of Administration report concluded the annexation was not in the public interest, citing concerns about water and sewer services. Village staff recommended rejection and QTS subsequently withdrew.
objective: Community pressure forces QTS to withdraw in DeForest

summary: Constellation Energy purchased over 600 acres near Essex, Illinois, and is seeking rezoning and annexation. Much of the land was sold by village board members or their relatives. The Essex Coalition launched a petition urging industrial protections and is working to revive the village's dormant zoning board of appeals. Constellation has not officially announced a data center but residents fear one is planned given the site's proximity to power infrastructure.
objective: Block Constellation Energy rezoning of 600 acres in Essex

opposition_groups: Sierra Club Illinois (Heart of Illinois Group)
summary: About two dozen south and southwest suburban residents gathered at a Frankfort, Illinois, forum organized by environmental advocates to voice concerns about data center proposals in Joliet, Minooka, Essex, Coal City, and Yorkville. Residents fear higher utility bills, regional power outages, declining air quality, and strained water supplies from the cluster of proposed facilities in the greater Will County area.
objective: Regional forum raises alarm over data center water, power, and air quality impacts near Frankfort

summary: Kosciusko County, Indiana, Area Plan Commission unanimously denied (8-0) Prologis's rezoning request for 554 acres of farmland near Leesburg from agricultural to industrial for a 10-building data center campus. County Commissioners upheld denial 3-0 on April 22. Over 100 residents attended, and a petition collected 1,000+ signatures. Residents cited loss of farmland and impact on the local Amish farming community.
objective: Deny Prologis rezoning of 554 acres of farmland

summary: Washington Township in northwestern Franklin County passed a 90-day moratorium on data centers in unincorporated parts of the township on December 8, 2025. The township also asked the city of Dublin, which the township encompasses, to adopt a moratorium as well. The action follows the broader wave of Central Ohio communities pushing back against the data center boom centered on New Albany.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Washington Township

summary: St. Albans Township, Ohio, held a public hearing on March 10, 2026 on a proposal to become the first township in Licking County to ban data centers. The township sits on the eastern edge of the New Albany data center corridor, home to 40+ data centers in western Licking County. Residents expressed concerns about water and electricity consumption from the nearby facilities. St. Albans Township became the first in Licking County to ban data centers. Public hearing held March 10 with 25 attendees. Trustee Mike Washington read the resolution and opened public comment. A court reporter documented testimony. The ban was approved.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in St. Albans Township

summary: Oppidan, an Excelsior-based developer, paused its data center project in Hampton after citing that the state would take too long to permit backup generators. Minnesota's permitting process for diesel backup generators takes 12-18 months. Amazon also abandoned its Becker data center after regulators required a major state permit for 250 diesel generators.
objective: Oppidan pauses Hampton data center over generator permitting delays

opposition_groups: Mishicot-area residents and family farms (ad hoc coalition)
summary: Two Creeks, along with Two Rivers and Mishicot, held a joint town board meeting and approved identical resolutions calling on Manitowoc County to adopt a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction, with option to extend 6 months (by Cloverleaf Infrastructure). These three northeast Wisconsin towns acted proactively to prevent data center siting in their communities.
objective: Impose 6 month data centers moratorium in Two Creeks

opposition_groups: Mishicot-area residents and family farms (ad hoc coalition)
summary: Two Rivers, Wisconsin, joined Two Creeks and Mishicot at a joint town board meeting on February 12, 2026 in approving identical resolutions calling for a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction in Manitowoc County, with option for a six-month extension. The action came after Texas-based Cloverleaf Infrastructure approached local farmers with offers to purchase land, with one Mishicot farmer reportedly offered $70-80 million for up to 6,000 acres. Over 70 residents attended the joint meeting, and the Manitowoc County Board's Park and Planning Commission took up the moratorium petition on March 30.
objective: Impose one-year moratorium on data center permits in Two Rivers, Two Creeks, and Mishicot

opposition_groups: Mishicot-area residents and family farms (ad hoc coalition)
summary: Mishicot, Wisconsin, joined Two Creeks and Two Rivers at a joint town board meeting after several hundred people packed the Grey Fox Bar and Grill on January 26, 2026 for an informational meeting on AI data centers, with organizers forced to change to a larger venue due to overwhelming turnout. Cloverleaf Infrastructure and NSI Land Services had been approaching local farmers to purchase property. Town Board Chair Dean Anhalt said residents are concerned about losing rural character and farmland, especially from retired farmers and landowners renting to active farming operations.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Mishicot

summary: The Janesville City Council voted unanimously on Feb 9, 2026 to place a citizen-initiated ballot measure on the November 3, 2026 general election ballot. The ordinance, organized by the No Janesville Data Center group, would require voter approval before the City could approve any development on undeveloped portions of the GM/JATCO site exceeding $450M. The 3,927 signatures were certified by the city clerk on January 21, 2026. The measure targets a Viridian Partners proposal to redevelop the site into an 800 MW, 11-building data center campus estimated at $8B. City Manager Kevin Lahner has warned the ordinance is likely illegal and unenforceable, citing 'constitutionality concerns.' The April 2026 council elections heavily centered on the data center question.
objective: Put Viridian data center to public vote

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
summary: The Saginaw area in Michigan is considering a 6-month moratorium on data center development. The Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan and Sierra Club Michigan Chapter organized opposition, raising concerns about the growing wave of AI data center proposals targeting Michigan communities. The moratorium outcome is pending.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Saginaw

summary: Bessemer, Alabama, City Council approved rezoning of 914+ acres from Agricultural to Light Industrial for a $14.5B hyperscale data center on November 18, 2025 (by Logistics Land Investments). Community members filed a lawsuit alleging inadequate public notice and 'arbitrary and capricious spot zoning.' Judge David Hobdy temporarily enjoined the city in August 2025, but the council proceeded with approval. The proposed campus expanded to 1,600 acres in updated plans (February 2026). ALDOT warned of conflicts with Northern Beltline highway plans. The case had a final hearing scheduled March 19, 2026.
objective: Challenge Logistics Land Investments rezoning in Bessemer in court

summary: Forsyth County, Georgia, enacted a strict data center ordinance that prohibits data center cooling systems from using the county water system, one of the most restrictive approaches among Georgia counties. The ordinance is part of a wave of local regulations across Georgia, with eight counties and cities adopting moratoria or ordinances since March 2025. Separately, a proposed $8.4B data center in the City of Forsyth (Monroe County) would require 1.5 million gallons of water per day, raising infrastructure strain concerns.
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Forsyth County

opposition_groups: Stanton Springs Data Center (opposition Facebook page)
summary: The City of Covington, Georgia, council imposed a 180-day moratorium on new data center requests on January 5, 2026 (by 3Rivers Development). During the moratorium, city staff will review current ordinances and make recommendations. Data centers have been seeking to annex into incorporated cities like Covington and Social Circle to bypass Newton County's stricter overlay requirements.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Covington

summary: Newton County Board of Commissioners unanimously enacted an emergency moratorium on data center applications in February 2026, joining a wave of Georgia moratoriums. Previously, SC Infrastructure / Technology Park Atlanta proposed a $5B, nine-building data center campus (2.6M sq ft on 317 acres) east of I-20 near Covington. The city of Covington enacted a similar moratorium in January 2026. Georgia state lawmakers debated data center regulation throughout their 2026 session but ultimately failed to pass any legislation.
objective: Pause data centers in Newton County pending regulations

summary: A 437-acre plot on the outskirts of the 3,000-person town of Hogansville, Georgia, is the center of a Fertile Ground Investments LLC data center debate. Residents are concerned about property values, water and energy consumption, and utility bills. City officials signed an NDA about the project at least 18 months before informing the public, angering residents who demanded transparency.
objective: Expose NDA-shrouded data center proposal on 437 acres in Hogansville

company: Meta (via Beignet LLC / Blue Owl Capital)
summary: Meta's Hyperion data center -- the company's largest -- is under construction in Richland Parish, Louisiana, requiring 2,000 MW and 5 million gallons of water per day from groundwater. Earthjustice filed motions against a risky 'Frankenstein financing' deal that could leave ratepayers paying for 3 new gas plants if Meta exits after 4 years. Low-income families were evicted from a trailer park for worker housing. Median home prices rose 80% since early 2024.
objective: Challenge Meta's 2000MW data center groundwater withdrawal

summary: SELC filed a lawsuit in January 2026 on behalf of Colleton County, South Carolina, residents challenging a Nov 2025 zoning ordinance that added data centers as a 'special exception' in rural zones including the environmentally sensitive ACE Basin. Eagle Rock Partners then proposed an 859-acre, 1,000 MW data center complex under the new ordinance. A bipartisan group of state legislators also drafted a resolution urging the county to challenge the project. Nine data centers total have been proposed in the county.
objective: Legal challenge to Eagle Rock Partners data center in Colleton County

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: On August 7, 2025, the Prince William County, Virginia, Circuit Court invalidated the Digital Gateway rezoning approvals, declaring the ordinances 'void ab initio' due to failure to comply with notice requirements (by QTS Realty Trust / Compass). The Court of Appeals held oral arguments on February 25, 2026. PWC has spent an additional $400K on defense. A written decision is pending.
objective: Challenge QTS Realty Trust / Compass rezoning in Prince William in court

summary: Protect Stafford, led by Erin Sanzero, achieved major zoning victories in 2025. After an initial setback in March (4-3 vote against conditional-use permit requirements), the Board of Supervisors adopted comprehensive data center regulations on October 21, 2025, including a 750-foot setback from residential property lines — the largest in Virginia, a 650-foot increase over the previous 100-foot setback. The board also adopted updated Comprehensive Plan guidelines. However, five pre-existing data center projects were grandfathered in December 2025, including Vantage's $2B VA4 campus.
objective: Win conditional use permit requirement and 750-ft setbacks via Protect Stafford coalition

summary: xAI operated 27 gas turbines at its Southaven, Mississippi, facility without air permits, violating the Clean Air Act according to the NAACP, which threatened a federal lawsuit (by xAI (MZX Tech)). At a 2.5-hour public hearing on Feb 17, 2026, not a single person spoke in favor of xAI's proposed permanent air permit for 40+ gas turbines. Residents reported respiratory problems and headache-inducing 24/7 noise. A local group 'Safe and Sound' documents decibel levels. Despite opposition, MDEQ approved the air permit on March 11, 2026.
objective: Challenge xAI (MZX Tech) air quality permits in court

summary: Governor Ferguson issued Executive Order 25-05 on February 3, 2025 creating the Data Center Workgroup, convened by the Department of Revenue with 33 members from state agencies, utilities, labor, environmental groups, and industry. The workgroup delivered its preliminary report on December 1, 2025, recommending data centers comply with Washington's 2045 emissions-free electricity mandate. The Washington Legislature punted comprehensive data center legislation to 2027. The workgroup's final report is expected later in 2026 and will inform the next legislative session.
objective: Form state workgroup on data center policy in WA

summary: Oregon CUB accused PGE of circumventing the POWER Act (by Portland General Electric). PGE built $174M in substations exclusively for data centers but proposed assigning 47% of costs to residential customers. CUB analysis shows PGE would allocate 34-45% of new supply/transmission costs to households despite data centers driving demand. PUC decision expected April 2026.
objective: Oppose transmission lines for Portland General Electric data center

summary: HR 655 would transfer ~150 acres of Mt. Hood National Forest to The Dalles to triple reservoir capacity. Passed House Dec 9, 2025; before Senate Energy Committee. Conservation coalition calls it a 'cynical sweetheart deal' to serve Google's growing water demands. City denies it's for Google but water plan references 'large industrial user' needing ~1M gallons/day.
objective: Transfer 150 acres of Mt. Hood National Forest to expand reservoir capacity for The Dalles

summary: Ottawa City Council established a 180-day moratorium on data centers across all zoning districts, the first such moratorium in the Illinois Valley. Mayor Robert Hasty cited the need for electrical, water, and sound guardrails before entertaining proposals, noting current zoning provides 'very little regulation' once companies receive state approvals. Commissioner Tom Ganiere supported the measure but urged it not be 'so restrictive that it scares any development away from the city.'
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Ottawa

summary: The City of Forney, Texas, is considering new zoning rules that would formally regulate data center development within city limits. A public hearing is scheduled for March 17, 2026 on a proposed ordinance amending Forney's Comprehensive Zoning Ordinance. The amendment would include a formal definition of a data center, establish specific development regulations, and require a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) in several zoning districts. Operational requirements include full enclosure of cooling equipment, generators, and other infrastructure. The Planning and Zoning Commission has already reviewed and recommended approval.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Forney

summary: Center for Coalfield Justice led a protest on March 12, 2026 chanting 'Our land, our power, our land, our water' as Washington County commissioners held an invite-only 'Data Center Happy Hour' with developers including Texas-based Prime Data Centers and Florida-based Next Generation Land Company inside the George Washington Hotel. Next Generation proposed a 150,000 sq ft facility in rural West, Pennsylvania, Finley, where residents rely on well water and aquifers. Protesters cited potential impacts on 23 dairy farms and warned data centers could consume up to 5 million gallons of water per day.
objective: Rally against Next Generation Land Company data center in West Finley Township

summary: Prince William County, Virginia, Planning Commission voted 5-3 on March 11, 2026 to deny the Diamond Hill substation — a proposed 300 MW electric utility substation with a collocated Dominion Energy switching station at 13301 Casey Lane in Bristow. NOVEC proposed the facility to power the adjacent Hunter Property data center campus, but it was sited immediately behind the Amberleigh Station residential community. Commissioners cited noise, health, farmland, and historic preservation concerns.
objective: Deny NOVEC 300MW data center permit in Prince William
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County

summary: Cambrin LLC, a shell company linked to Meta, proposed a data center on approximately 430 acres in the Town of Beloit, Wisconsin. The 'No Beloit Data Center' opposition group organized community resistance, raising concerns about the project's impact on the community. The outcome is pending. Key concerns driving this action include transparency requirements and loss of farmland.
objective: Oppose Cambrin (Meta) data center in Town of Beloit

summary: Brazoria County, Texas, commissioners unanimously rejected a package of tax abatements sought by Nightpeak Energy for its proposed $3.5 billion Old Ocean Data Center campus outside Sweeny, about 65 miles southwest of Houston. The project calls for two 310-megawatt hyperscale data centers paired with two 310-megawatt combustion turbine power plants on a 158-acre site. Each data center carries a projected price tag of roughly $1.5 billion, while the power plants would cost about $250 million apiece. Commissioners declined the incentives after residents and local officials raised concerns about noise, water consumption and power demand.
